{{Original research|date=January 2011}} '''Temples and Shrines''' (寺社, Jisha) is a collective term in Japan referring to both {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}} and {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}. It is also called '''Shrines and Temples''' (社寺, Shaji) or '''Shrines and Temple Precincts''' (神社仏閣, Jinja Bukkaku).
The term "Jisha" reflects the idea of Buddhist supremacy over Shinto that prevailed until the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. On the other hand, the term "Shaji" appeared as early as the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, primarily reflecting a preference for shrines and was widely used after the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}.
== Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism ==
Both temples and shrines (and their deities, {{ill|kami|ja|神}} and {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏}}) have been objects of faith in Japan, and prior to the {{ill|separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離}} during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, they were often treated collectively without distinction when not necessary.
The history of {{ill|syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} lies in the blending of native {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} beliefs and imported {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}} (especially {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}), which gave rise to diverse faiths like {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道}}. Theoretical frameworks like the {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹}} doctrine attempted to unify these practices, with "Gongen" (権現) serving as an ambiguous term representing both. Examples of architectural integration include "Jingūji" (神宮寺, Shrine-Temples).
During the Meiji period's separation policies, syncretized entities were forcibly divided into either shrines or temples, often resulting in the abolition of one side.
However, at the grassroots level, these changes caused minimal shifts in popular consciousness. Local {{ill|folk religion|lt=folk religions|ja|民間信仰}} and {{ill|festivals|ja|祭り}} continued relatively unchanged, preserving traditions beyond the scope of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}}.
In modern times, the differences between temples and shrines are seldom emphasized outside specific rituals like funerals. Japanese people are often described as having a "weak sense of faith," with temples and shrines viewed more for their {{ill|worldly benefits|ja|現世利益}} or as {{ill|tourist destinations|ja|観光地}}.
==Articles with this term in their title== {{ill|Temple and Shrine Estates|ja|寺社領}} Temple and Shrine Estates (じしゃりょう) refer to one of the territorial divisions that existed in Japan in the past. Primarily, these estates were established to support the maintenance and operation of temples and shrines. In ancient times, revenue sources for temples and shrines included self-owned lands (shinden and teraden), grants from the ritsuryō state, and early shōen estates developed through reclamation efforts. Some major temples and shrines held extensive territories that provided significant income.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Magistrate|ja|寺社奉行}} The Temple and Shrine Magistrate (じしゃぶぎょう) was an official position within the military governments from the Muromachi period through the Edo period, responsible for overseeing religious administration. Originating in the Kamakura shogunate, this office managed temple and shrine estates, buildings, monks, and shrine priests. The Edo shogunate established this position in 1635 (Kan'ei 12), placing it under the direct authority of the shogun and ranking it highest among the three magistrates. The magistrate also oversaw other cultural and administrative aspects related to religious institutions.
{{ill|Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line|ja|新潟県道590号保田寺社線}} Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line (にいがたけんどう590ごう やすだじしゃせん) is a general prefectural road in Agano City, Niigata Prefecture. It serves as an access route from National Route 49 to the Niigata Eastern Industrial Complex. - Starting point: Yasuda, Agano City, Niigata Prefecture (intersection with Niigata Prefectural Route 55) - Endpoint: Jisha, Agano City (intersection with National Route 49)
{{ill|Miyadaiku|ja|宮大工}} (redirect from {{ill|Temple and Shrine Carpenter|ja|寺社大工}}) Miyadaiku refers to master carpenters specializing in the construction and restoration of temples and shrines. These craftsmen maintain unique traditional techniques, including the use of yari kanna (a spear-plane), which has largely disappeared from general carpentry. Only a handful of specialist companies, such as Takigawa Jisha Architecture in Nara, are entrusted with the restoration of National Treasure and Important Cultural Property buildings.
{{ill|Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records|ja|大乗院寺社雑事記}} The Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records (だいじょういんじしゃぞうじき) is a diary written over three generations by Jinson, Shōkaku, and Kyōjin, monks who managed Kōfuku-ji’s Daijōin in the Muromachi period. Spanning approximately 190 volumes, the surviving records from 1450 (Hōtoku 2) to 1527 (Daiei 7) are preserved in the National Archives of Japan and designated as Important Cultural Property.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Power|ja|寺社勢力}} The Temple and Shrine Power (じしゃせいりょく) refers to the military, administrative, economic, and cultural influence of large temples and shrines in medieval Japan. Alongside the military government and the imperial court, religious institutions played a central role in politics and governance. Due to the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, many major religious sites wielded substantial territorial control and maintained private armies.
{{ill|Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|ja|京都寺社等同時放火事件}} The Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident was a series of arson attacks that took place in Kyoto on April 24-25, 1993. A senior member of the Japanese New Leftist group Chūkakuha was arrested and charged as the suspected perpetrator, but was later acquitted, with the ruling becoming final.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala|ja|社寺参詣曼荼羅}} A Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala is a religious painting created to depict sacred sites, such as temples and shrines, for the purpose of promoting pilgrimage and religious devotion. Many surviving examples were painted on paper and used in religious fundraising campaigns.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Honjoryō|ja|寺社本所領}} Temple and Shrine Honjoryō refers to the estates belonging to religious institutions in contrast to those controlled by the warrior class. Before the 12th century, there was no clear distinction, but as the Kamakura shogunate strengthened its authority, temple and shrine lands were increasingly categorized separately from warrior-held territories.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Regulations|ja|寺社法度}} Temple Regulations were a set of legal codes used by the Edo shogunate and local lords to govern religious institutions. These laws often granted temple and shrine lands while imposing various controls, such as restrictions on logging within temple and shrine precincts.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|ja|寺社興行法}} The Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws refer to legal measures implemented by secular authorities to restore and regulate religious ceremonies. These laws aimed to revive religious practices that had declined due to various circumstances, particularly from the late Heian period onward.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Law|ja|寺社法}} Temple Law, also known as Temple and Shrine Law, refers to the legal framework governing religious institutions in premodern Japan. This framework involved two main types of authority: secular control over religious institutions and self-regulation by the religious communities themselves.
== See Also == * {{ill|Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} * {{ill|Temple and Shrine Authorities|ja|寺社勢力}} * {{ill|Magistrates for Temples and Shrines|ja|寺社奉行}} * {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} * {{ill|List of Temples in Japan|ja|日本の寺院一覧}} * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Japan|ja|神社一覧}}

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Completion tier 1]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Original research|date=January 2011}} '''Temples and Shrines''' (寺社, Jisha) is a collective term in Japan referring to both {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}} and {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}. It is also called '''Shrines and Temples''' (社寺, Shaji) or '''Shrines and Temple Precincts''' (神社仏閣, Jinja Bukkaku).
The term "Jisha" reflects the idea of Buddhist supremacy over Shinto that prevailed until the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. On the other hand, the term "Shaji" appeared as early as the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, primarily reflecting a preference for shrines and was widely used after the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}.
== Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism ==
Both temples and shrines (and their deities, {{ill|kami|ja|神}} and {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏}}) have been objects of faith in Japan, and prior to the {{ill|separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離}} during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, they were often treated collectively without distinction when not necessary.
The history of {{ill|syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} lies in the blending of native {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} beliefs and imported {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}} (especially {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}), which gave rise to diverse faiths like {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道}}. Theoretical frameworks like the {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹}} doctrine attempted to unify these practices, with "Gongen" (権現) serving as an ambiguous term representing both. Examples of architectural integration include "Jingūji" (神宮寺, Shrine-Temples).
During the Meiji period's separation policies, syncretized entities were forcibly divided into either shrines or temples, often resulting in the abolition of one side.
However, at the grassroots level, these changes caused minimal shifts in popular consciousness. Local {{ill|folk religion|lt=folk religions|ja|民間信仰}} and {{ill|festivals|ja|祭り}} continued relatively unchanged, preserving traditions beyond the scope of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}}.
In modern times, the differences between temples and shrines are seldom emphasized outside specific rituals like funerals. Japanese people are often described as having a "weak sense of faith," with temples and shrines viewed more for their {{ill|worldly benefits|ja|現世利益}} or as {{ill|tourist destinations|ja|観光地}}.
==Articles with this term in their title== {{ill|Temple and Shrine Estates|ja|寺社領}} Temple and Shrine Estates (じしゃりょう) refer to one of the territorial divisions that existed in Japan in the past. Primarily, these estates were established to support the maintenance and operation of temples and shrines. In ancient times, revenue sources for temples and shrines included self-owned lands (shinden and teraden), grants from the ritsuryō state, and early shōen estates developed through reclamation efforts. Some major temples and shrines held extensive territories that provided significant income.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Magistrate|ja|寺社奉行}} The Temple and Shrine Magistrate (じしゃぶぎょう) was an official position within the military governments from the Muromachi period through the Edo period, responsible for overseeing religious administration. Originating in the Kamakura shogunate, this office managed temple and shrine estates, buildings, monks, and shrine priests. The Edo shogunate established this position in 1635 (Kan'ei 12), placing it under the direct authority of the shogun and ranking it highest among the three magistrates. The magistrate also oversaw other cultural and administrative aspects related to religious institutions.
{{ill|Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line|ja|新潟県道590号保田寺社線}} Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line (にいがたけんどう590ごう やすだじしゃせん) is a general prefectural road in Agano City, Niigata Prefecture. It serves as an access route from National Route 49 to the Niigata Eastern Industrial Complex. - Starting point: Yasuda, Agano City, Niigata Prefecture (intersection with Niigata Prefectural Route 55) - Endpoint: Jisha, Agano City (intersection with National Route 49)
{{ill|Miyadaiku|ja|宮大工}} (redirect from {{ill|Temple and Shrine Carpenter|ja|寺社大工}}) Miyadaiku refers to master carpenters specializing in the construction and restoration of temples and shrines. These craftsmen maintain unique traditional techniques, including the use of yari kanna (a spear-plane), which has largely disappeared from general carpentry. Only a handful of specialist companies, such as Takigawa Jisha Architecture in Nara, are entrusted with the restoration of National Treasure and Important Cultural Property buildings.
{{ill|Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records|ja|大乗院寺社雑事記}} The Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records (だいじょういんじしゃぞうじき) is a diary written over three generations by Jinson, Shōkaku, and Kyōjin, monks who managed Kōfuku-ji’s Daijōin in the Muromachi period. Spanning approximately 190 volumes, the surviving records from 1450 (Hōtoku 2) to 1527 (Daiei 7) are preserved in the National Archives of Japan and designated as Important Cultural Property.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Power|ja|寺社勢力}} The Temple and Shrine Power (じしゃせいりょく) refers to the military, administrative, economic, and cultural influence of large temples and shrines in medieval Japan. Alongside the military government and the imperial court, religious institutions played a central role in politics and governance. Due to the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, many major religious sites wielded substantial territorial control and maintained private armies.
{{ill|Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|ja|京都寺社等同時放火事件}} The Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident was a series of arson attacks that took place in Kyoto on April 24-25, 1993. A senior member of the Japanese New Leftist group Chūkakuha was arrested and charged as the suspected perpetrator, but was later acquitted, with the ruling becoming final.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala|ja|社寺参詣曼荼羅}} A Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala is a religious painting created to depict sacred sites, such as temples and shrines, for the purpose of promoting pilgrimage and religious devotion. Many surviving examples were painted on paper and used in religious fundraising campaigns.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Honjoryō|ja|寺社本所領}} Temple and Shrine Honjoryō refers to the estates belonging to religious institutions in contrast to those controlled by the warrior class. Before the 12th century, there was no clear distinction, but as the Kamakura shogunate strengthened its authority, temple and shrine lands were increasingly categorized separately from warrior-held territories.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Regulations|ja|寺社法度}} Temple Regulations were a set of legal codes used by the Edo shogunate and local lords to govern religious institutions. These laws often granted temple and shrine lands while imposing various controls, such as restrictions on logging within temple and shrine precincts.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|ja|寺社興行法}} The Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws refer to legal measures implemented by secular authorities to restore and regulate religious ceremonies. These laws aimed to revive religious practices that had declined due to various circumstances, particularly from the late Heian period onward.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Law|ja|寺社法}} Temple Law, also known as Temple and Shrine Law, refers to the legal framework governing religious institutions in premodern Japan. This framework involved two main types of authority: secular control over religious institutions and self-regulation by the religious communities themselves.
== See Also == * {{ill|Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} * {{ill|Temple and Shrine Authorities|ja|寺社勢力}} * {{ill|Magistrates for Temples and Shrines|ja|寺社奉行}} * {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} * {{ill|List of Temples in Japan|ja|日本の寺院一覧}} * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Japan|ja|神社一覧}}

{{Original research|date=January 2011}} '''Temples and Shrines''' (寺社, Jisha) is a collective term in Japan referring to both {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}} and {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}. It is also called '''Shrines and Temples''' (社寺, Shaji) or '''Shrines and Temple Precincts''' (神社仏閣, Jinja Bukkaku).
The term "Jisha" reflects the idea of Buddhist supremacy over Shinto that prevailed until the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. On the other hand, the term "Shaji" appeared as early as the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, primarily reflecting a preference for shrines and was widely used after the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}.
== Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism ==
Both temples and shrines (and their deities, {{ill|kami|ja|神}} and {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏}}) have been objects of faith in Japan, and prior to the {{ill|separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離}} during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, they were often treated collectively without distinction when not necessary.
The history of {{ill|syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} lies in the blending of native {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} beliefs and imported {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}} (especially {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}), which gave rise to diverse faiths like {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道}}. Theoretical frameworks like the {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹}} doctrine attempted to unify these practices, with "Gongen" (権現) serving as an ambiguous term representing both. Examples of architectural integration include "Jingūji" (神宮寺, Shrine-Temples).
During the Meiji period's separation policies, syncretized entities were forcibly divided into either shrines or temples, often resulting in the abolition of one side.
However, at the grassroots level, these changes caused minimal shifts in popular consciousness. Local {{ill|folk religion|lt=folk religions|ja|民間信仰}} and {{ill|festivals|ja|祭り}} continued relatively unchanged, preserving traditions beyond the scope of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}}.
In modern times, the differences between temples and shrines are seldom emphasized outside specific rituals like funerals. Japanese people are often described as having a "weak sense of faith," with temples and shrines viewed more for their {{ill|worldly benefits|ja|現世利益}} or as {{ill|tourist destinations|ja|観光地}}.
==Articles with this term in their title== {{ill|Temple and Shrine Estates|ja|寺社領}} Temple and Shrine Estates (じしゃりょう) refer to one of the territorial divisions that existed in Japan in the past. Primarily, these estates were established to support the maintenance and operation of temples and shrines. In ancient times, revenue sources for temples and shrines included self-owned lands (shinden and teraden), grants from the ritsuryō state, and early shōen estates developed through reclamation efforts. Some major temples and shrines held extensive territories that provided significant income.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Magistrate|ja|寺社奉行}} The Temple and Shrine Magistrate (じしゃぶぎょう) was an official position within the military governments from the Muromachi period through the Edo period, responsible for overseeing religious administration. Originating in the Kamakura shogunate, this office managed temple and shrine estates, buildings, monks, and shrine priests. The Edo shogunate established this position in 1635 (Kan'ei 12), placing it under the direct authority of the shogun and ranking it highest among the three magistrates. The magistrate also oversaw other cultural and administrative aspects related to religious institutions.
{{ill|Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line|ja|新潟県道590号保田寺社線}} Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line (にいがたけんどう590ごう やすだじしゃせん) is a general prefectural road in Agano City, Niigata Prefecture. It serves as an access route from National Route 49 to the Niigata Eastern Industrial Complex. - Starting point: Yasuda, Agano City, Niigata Prefecture (intersection with Niigata Prefectural Route 55) - Endpoint: Jisha, Agano City (intersection with National Route 49)
{{ill|Miyadaiku|ja|宮大工}} (redirect from {{ill|Temple and Shrine Carpenter|ja|寺社大工}}) Miyadaiku refers to master carpenters specializing in the construction and restoration of temples and shrines. These craftsmen maintain unique traditional techniques, including the use of yari kanna (a spear-plane), which has largely disappeared from general carpentry. Only a handful of specialist companies, such as Takigawa Jisha Architecture in Nara, are entrusted with the restoration of National Treasure and Important Cultural Property buildings.
{{ill|Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records|ja|大乗院寺社雑事記}} The Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records (だいじょういんじしゃぞうじき) is a diary written over three generations by Jinson, Shōkaku, and Kyōjin, monks who managed Kōfuku-ji’s Daijōin in the Muromachi period. Spanning approximately 190 volumes, the surviving records from 1450 (Hōtoku 2) to 1527 (Daiei 7) are preserved in the National Archives of Japan and designated as Important Cultural Property.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Power|ja|寺社勢力}} The Temple and Shrine Power (じしゃせいりょく) refers to the military, administrative, economic, and cultural influence of large temples and shrines in medieval Japan. Alongside the military government and the imperial court, religious institutions played a central role in politics and governance. Due to the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, many major religious sites wielded substantial territorial control and maintained private armies.
{{ill|Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|ja|京都寺社等同時放火事件}} The Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident was a series of arson attacks that took place in Kyoto on April 24-25, 1993. A senior member of the Japanese New Leftist group Chūkakuha was arrested and charged as the suspected perpetrator, but was later acquitted, with the ruling becoming final.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala|ja|社寺参詣曼荼羅}} A Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala is a religious painting created to depict sacred sites, such as temples and shrines, for the purpose of promoting pilgrimage and religious devotion. Many surviving examples were painted on paper and used in religious fundraising campaigns.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Honjoryō|ja|寺社本所領}} Temple and Shrine Honjoryō refers to the estates belonging to religious institutions in contrast to those controlled by the warrior class. Before the 12th century, there was no clear distinction, but as the Kamakura shogunate strengthened its authority, temple and shrine lands were increasingly categorized separately from warrior-held territories.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Regulations|ja|寺社法度}} Temple Regulations were a set of legal codes used by the Edo shogunate and local lords to govern religious institutions. These laws often granted temple and shrine lands while imposing various controls, such as restrictions on logging within temple and shrine precincts.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|ja|寺社興行法}} The Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws refer to legal measures implemented by secular authorities to restore and regulate religious ceremonies. These laws aimed to revive religious practices that had declined due to various circumstances, particularly from the late Heian period onward.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Law|ja|寺社法}} Temple Law, also known as Temple and Shrine Law, refers to the legal framework governing religious institutions in premodern Japan. This framework involved two main types of authority: secular control over religious institutions and self-regulation by the religious communities themselves.
== See Also == * {{ill|Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} * {{ill|Temple and Shrine Authorities|ja|寺社勢力}} * {{ill|Magistrates for Temples and Shrines|ja|寺社奉行}} * {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} * {{ill|List of Temples in Japan|ja|日本の寺院一覧}} * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Japan|ja|神社一覧}}

revision.text:
{{Original research|date=January 2011}} '''Temples and Shrines''' (寺社, Jisha) is a collective term in Japan referring to both {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}} and {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}. It is also called '''Shrines and Temples''' (社寺, Shaji) or '''Shrines and Temple Precincts''' (神社仏閣, Jinja Bukkaku).
The term "Jisha" reflects the idea of Buddhist supremacy over Shinto that prevailed until the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. On the other hand, the term "Shaji" appeared as early as the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, primarily reflecting a preference for shrines and was widely used after the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}.
== Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism ==
Both temples and shrines (and their deities, {{ill|kami|ja|神}} and {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏}}) have been objects of faith in Japan, and prior to the {{ill|separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離}} during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, they were often treated collectively without distinction when not necessary.
The history of {{ill|syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} lies in the blending of native {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} beliefs and imported {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}} (especially {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}), which gave rise to diverse faiths like {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道}}. Theoretical frameworks like the {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹}} doctrine attempted to unify these practices, with "Gongen" (権現) serving as an ambiguous term representing both. Examples of architectural integration include "Jingūji" (神宮寺, Shrine-Temples).
During the Meiji period's separation policies, syncretized entities were forcibly divided into either shrines or temples, often resulting in the abolition of one side.
However, at the grassroots level, these changes caused minimal shifts in popular consciousness. Local {{ill|folk religion|lt=folk religions|ja|民間信仰}} and {{ill|festivals|ja|祭り}} continued relatively unchanged, preserving traditions beyond the scope of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}}.
In modern times, the differences between temples and shrines are seldom emphasized outside specific rituals like funerals. Japanese people are often described as having a "weak sense of faith," with temples and shrines viewed more for their {{ill|worldly benefits|ja|現世利益}} or as {{ill|tourist destinations|ja|観光地}}.
==Articles with this term in their title== {{ill|Temple and Shrine Estates|ja|寺社領}} Temple and Shrine Estates (じしゃりょう) refer to one of the territorial divisions that existed in Japan in the past. Primarily, these estates were established to support the maintenance and operation of temples and shrines. In ancient times, revenue sources for temples and shrines included self-owned lands (shinden and teraden), grants from the ritsuryō state, and early shōen estates developed through reclamation efforts. Some major temples and shrines held extensive territories that provided significant income.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Magistrate|ja|寺社奉行}} The Temple and Shrine Magistrate (じしゃぶぎょう) was an official position within the military governments from the Muromachi period through the Edo period, responsible for overseeing religious administration. Originating in the Kamakura shogunate, this office managed temple and shrine estates, buildings, monks, and shrine priests. The Edo shogunate established this position in 1635 (Kan'ei 12), placing it under the direct authority of the shogun and ranking it highest among the three magistrates. The magistrate also oversaw other cultural and administrative aspects related to religious institutions.
{{ill|Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line|ja|新潟県道590号保田寺社線}} Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line (にいがたけんどう590ごう やすだじしゃせん) is a general prefectural road in Agano City, Niigata Prefecture. It serves as an access route from National Route 49 to the Niigata Eastern Industrial Complex. - Starting point: Yasuda, Agano City, Niigata Prefecture (intersection with Niigata Prefectural Route 55) - Endpoint: Jisha, Agano City (intersection with National Route 49)
{{ill|Miyadaiku|ja|宮大工}} (redirect from {{ill|Temple and Shrine Carpenter|ja|寺社大工}}) Miyadaiku refers to master carpenters specializing in the construction and restoration of temples and shrines. These craftsmen maintain unique traditional techniques, including the use of yari kanna (a spear-plane), which has largely disappeared from general carpentry. Only a handful of specialist companies, such as Takigawa Jisha Architecture in Nara, are entrusted with the restoration of National Treasure and Important Cultural Property buildings.
{{ill|Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records|ja|大乗院寺社雑事記}} The Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records (だいじょういんじしゃぞうじき) is a diary written over three generations by Jinson, Shōkaku, and Kyōjin, monks who managed Kōfuku-ji’s Daijōin in the Muromachi period. Spanning approximately 190 volumes, the surviving records from 1450 (Hōtoku 2) to 1527 (Daiei 7) are preserved in the National Archives of Japan and designated as Important Cultural Property.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Power|ja|寺社勢力}} The Temple and Shrine Power (じしゃせいりょく) refers to the military, administrative, economic, and cultural influence of large temples and shrines in medieval Japan. Alongside the military government and the imperial court, religious institutions played a central role in politics and governance. Due to the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, many major religious sites wielded substantial territorial control and maintained private armies.
{{ill|Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|ja|京都寺社等同時放火事件}} The Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident was a series of arson attacks that took place in Kyoto on April 24-25, 1993. A senior member of the Japanese New Leftist group Chūkakuha was arrested and charged as the suspected perpetrator, but was later acquitted, with the ruling becoming final.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala|ja|社寺参詣曼荼羅}} A Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala is a religious painting created to depict sacred sites, such as temples and shrines, for the purpose of promoting pilgrimage and religious devotion. Many surviving examples were painted on paper and used in religious fundraising campaigns.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Honjoryō|ja|寺社本所領}} Temple and Shrine Honjoryō refers to the estates belonging to religious institutions in contrast to those controlled by the warrior class. Before the 12th century, there was no clear distinction, but as the Kamakura shogunate strengthened its authority, temple and shrine lands were increasingly categorized separately from warrior-held territories.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Regulations|ja|寺社法度}} Temple Regulations were a set of legal codes used by the Edo shogunate and local lords to govern religious institutions. These laws often granted temple and shrine lands while imposing various controls, such as restrictions on logging within temple and shrine precincts.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|ja|寺社興行法}} The Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws refer to legal measures implemented by secular authorities to restore and regulate religious ceremonies. These laws aimed to revive religious practices that had declined due to various circumstances, particularly from the late Heian period onward.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Law|ja|寺社法}} Temple Law, also known as Temple and Shrine Law, refers to the legal framework governing religious institutions in premodern Japan. This framework involved two main types of authority: secular control over religious institutions and self-regulation by the religious communities themselves.
== See Also == * {{ill|Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} * {{ill|Temple and Shrine Authorities|ja|寺社勢力}} * {{ill|Magistrates for Temples and Shrines|ja|寺社奉行}} * {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} * {{ill|List of Temples in Japan|ja|日本の寺院一覧}} * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Japan|ja|神社一覧}}

{{Original research|date=January 2011}} '''Temples and Shrines''' (寺社, Jisha) is a collective term in Japan referring to both {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}} and {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}. It is also called '''Shrines and Temples''' (社寺, Shaji) or '''Shrines and Temple Precincts''' (神社仏閣, Jinja Bukkaku).
The term "Jisha" reflects the idea of Buddhist supremacy over Shinto that prevailed until the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. On the other hand, the term "Shaji" appeared as early as the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, primarily reflecting a preference for shrines and was widely used after the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}.
== Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism ==
Both temples and shrines (and their deities, {{ill|kami|ja|神}} and {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏}}) have been objects of faith in Japan, and prior to the {{ill|separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離}} during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, they were often treated collectively without distinction when not necessary.
The history of {{ill|syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} lies in the blending of native {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} beliefs and imported {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}} (especially {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}), which gave rise to diverse faiths like {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道}}. Theoretical frameworks like the {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹}} doctrine attempted to unify these practices, with "Gongen" (権現) serving as an ambiguous term representing both. Examples of architectural integration include "Jingūji" (神宮寺, Shrine-Temples).
During the Meiji period's separation policies, syncretized entities were forcibly divided into either shrines or temples, often resulting in the abolition of one side.
However, at the grassroots level, these changes caused minimal shifts in popular consciousness. Local {{ill|folk religion|lt=folk religions|ja|民間信仰}} and {{ill|festivals|ja|祭り}} continued relatively unchanged, preserving traditions beyond the scope of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}}.
In modern times, the differences between temples and shrines are seldom emphasized outside specific rituals like funerals. Japanese people are often described as having a "weak sense of faith," with temples and shrines viewed more for their {{ill|worldly benefits|ja|現世利益}} or as {{ill|tourist destinations|ja|観光地}}.
==Articles with this term in their title== {{ill|Temple and Shrine Estates|ja|寺社領}} Temple and Shrine Estates (じしゃりょう) refer to one of the territorial divisions that existed in Japan in the past. Primarily, these estates were established to support the maintenance and operation of temples and shrines. In ancient times, revenue sources for temples and shrines included self-owned lands (shinden and teraden), grants from the ritsuryō state, and early shōen estates developed through reclamation efforts. Some major temples and shrines held extensive territories that provided significant income.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Magistrate|ja|寺社奉行}} The Temple and Shrine Magistrate (じしゃぶぎょう) was an official position within the military governments from the Muromachi period through the Edo period, responsible for overseeing religious administration. Originating in the Kamakura shogunate, this office managed temple and shrine estates, buildings, monks, and shrine priests. The Edo shogunate established this position in 1635 (Kan'ei 12), placing it under the direct authority of the shogun and ranking it highest among the three magistrates. The magistrate also oversaw other cultural and administrative aspects related to religious institutions.
{{ill|Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line|ja|新潟県道590号保田寺社線}} Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line (にいがたけんどう590ごう やすだじしゃせん) is a general prefectural road in Agano City, Niigata Prefecture. It serves as an access route from National Route 49 to the Niigata Eastern Industrial Complex. - Starting point: Yasuda, Agano City, Niigata Prefecture (intersection with Niigata Prefectural Route 55) - Endpoint: Jisha, Agano City (intersection with National Route 49)
{{ill|Miyadaiku|ja|宮大工}} (redirect from {{ill|Temple and Shrine Carpenter|ja|寺社大工}}) Miyadaiku refers to master carpenters specializing in the construction and restoration of temples and shrines. These craftsmen maintain unique traditional techniques, including the use of yari kanna (a spear-plane), which has largely disappeared from general carpentry. Only a handful of specialist companies, such as Takigawa Jisha Architecture in Nara, are entrusted with the restoration of National Treasure and Important Cultural Property buildings.
{{ill|Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records|ja|大乗院寺社雑事記}} The Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records (だいじょういんじしゃぞうじき) is a diary written over three generations by Jinson, Shōkaku, and Kyōjin, monks who managed Kōfuku-ji’s Daijōin in the Muromachi period. Spanning approximately 190 volumes, the surviving records from 1450 (Hōtoku 2) to 1527 (Daiei 7) are preserved in the National Archives of Japan and designated as Important Cultural Property.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Power|ja|寺社勢力}} The Temple and Shrine Power (じしゃせいりょく) refers to the military, administrative, economic, and cultural influence of large temples and shrines in medieval Japan. Alongside the military government and the imperial court, religious institutions played a central role in politics and governance. Due to the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, many major religious sites wielded substantial territorial control and maintained private armies.
{{ill|Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|ja|京都寺社等同時放火事件}} The Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident was a series of arson attacks that took place in Kyoto on April 24-25, 1993. A senior member of the Japanese New Leftist group Chūkakuha was arrested and charged as the suspected perpetrator, but was later acquitted, with the ruling becoming final.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala|ja|社寺参詣曼荼羅}} A Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala is a religious painting created to depict sacred sites, such as temples and shrines, for the purpose of promoting pilgrimage and religious devotion. Many surviving examples were painted on paper and used in religious fundraising campaigns.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Honjoryō|ja|寺社本所領}} Temple and Shrine Honjoryō refers to the estates belonging to religious institutions in contrast to those controlled by the warrior class. Before the 12th century, there was no clear distinction, but as the Kamakura shogunate strengthened its authority, temple and shrine lands were increasingly categorized separately from warrior-held territories.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Regulations|ja|寺社法度}} Temple Regulations were a set of legal codes used by the Edo shogunate and local lords to govern religious institutions. These laws often granted temple and shrine lands while imposing various controls, such as restrictions on logging within temple and shrine precincts.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|ja|寺社興行法}} The Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws refer to legal measures implemented by secular authorities to restore and regulate religious ceremonies. These laws aimed to revive religious practices that had declined due to various circumstances, particularly from the late Heian period onward.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Law|ja|寺社法}} Temple Law, also known as Temple and Shrine Law, refers to the legal framework governing religious institutions in premodern Japan. This framework involved two main types of authority: secular control over religious institutions and self-regulation by the religious communities themselves.

{{Original research|date=January 2011}} '''Temples and Shrines''' (寺社, Jisha) is a collective term in Japan referring to both {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}} and {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}. It is also called '''Shrines and Temples''' (社寺, Shaji) or '''Shrines and Temple Precincts''' (神社仏閣, Jinja Bukkaku).
The term "Jisha" reflects the idea of Buddhist supremacy over Shinto that prevailed until the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. On the other hand, the term "Shaji" appeared as early as the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, primarily reflecting a preference for shrines and was widely used after the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}.
== Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism ==
Both temples and shrines (and their deities, {{ill|kami|ja|神}} and {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏}}) have been objects of faith in Japan, and prior to the {{ill|separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離}} during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, they were often treated collectively without distinction when not necessary.
The history of {{ill|syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} lies in the blending of native {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} beliefs and imported {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}} (especially {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}), which gave rise to diverse faiths like {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道}}. Theoretical frameworks like the {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹}} doctrine attempted to unify these practices, with "Gongen" (権現) serving as an ambiguous term representing both. Examples of architectural integration include "Jingūji" (神宮寺, Shrine-Temples).
During the Meiji period's separation policies, syncretized entities were forcibly divided into either shrines or temples, often resulting in the abolition of one side.
However, at the grassroots level, these changes caused minimal shifts in popular consciousness. Local {{ill|folk religion|lt=folk religions|ja|民間信仰}} and {{ill|festivals|ja|祭り}} continued relatively unchanged, preserving traditions beyond the scope of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}}.
In modern times, the differences between temples and shrines are seldom emphasized outside specific rituals like funerals. Japanese people are often described as having a "weak sense of faith," with temples and shrines viewed more for their {{ill|worldly benefits|ja|現世利益}} or as {{ill|tourist destinations|ja|観光地}}.
==Articles with this term in their title== {{ill|Temple and Shrine Estates|ja|寺社領}} Temple and Shrine Estates (じしゃりょう) refer to one of the territorial divisions that existed in Japan in the past. Primarily, these estates were established to support the maintenance and operation of temples and shrines. In ancient times, revenue sources for temples and shrines included self-owned lands (shinden and teraden), grants from the ritsuryō state, and early shōen estates developed through reclamation efforts. Some major temples and shrines held extensive territories that provided significant income.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Magistrate|ja|寺社奉行}} The Temple and Shrine Magistrate (じしゃぶぎょう) was an official position within the military governments from the Muromachi period through the Edo period, responsible for overseeing religious administration. Originating in the Kamakura shogunate, this office managed temple and shrine estates, buildings, monks, and shrine priests. The Edo shogunate established this position in 1635 (Kan'ei 12), placing it under the direct authority of the shogun and ranking it highest among the three magistrates. The magistrate also oversaw other cultural and administrative aspects related to religious institutions.
{{ill|Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line|ja|新潟県道590号保田寺社線}} Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line (にいがたけんどう590ごう やすだじしゃせん) is a general prefectural road in Agano City, Niigata Prefecture. It serves as an access route from National Route 49 to the Niigata Eastern Industrial Complex. - Starting point: Yasuda, Agano City, Niigata Prefecture (intersection with Niigata Prefectural Route 55) - Endpoint: Jisha, Agano City (intersection with National Route 49)
{{ill|Miyadaiku|ja|宮大工}} (redirect from {{ill|Temple and Shrine Carpenter|ja|寺社大工}}) Miyadaiku refers to master carpenters specializing in the construction and restoration of temples and shrines. These craftsmen maintain unique traditional techniques, including the use of yari kanna (a spear-plane), which has largely disappeared from general carpentry. Only a handful of specialist companies, such as Takigawa Jisha Architecture in Nara, are entrusted with the restoration of National Treasure and Important Cultural Property buildings.
{{ill|Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records|ja|大乗院寺社雑事記}} The Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records (だいじょういんじしゃぞうじき) is a diary written over three generations by Jinson, Shōkaku, and Kyōjin, monks who managed Kōfuku-ji’s Daijōin in the Muromachi period. Spanning approximately 190 volumes, the surviving records from 1450 (Hōtoku 2) to 1527 (Daiei 7) are preserved in the National Archives of Japan and designated as Important Cultural Property.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Power|ja|寺社勢力}} The Temple and Shrine Power (じしゃせいりょく) refers to the military, administrative, economic, and cultural influence of large temples and shrines in medieval Japan. Alongside the military government and the imperial court, religious institutions played a central role in politics and governance. Due to the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, many major religious sites wielded substantial territorial control and maintained private armies.
{{ill|Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|ja|京都寺社等同時放火事件}} The Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident was a series of arson attacks that took place in Kyoto on April 24-25, 1993. A senior member of the Japanese New Leftist group Chūkakuha was arrested and charged as the suspected perpetrator, but was later acquitted, with the ruling becoming final.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala|ja|社寺参詣曼荼羅}} A Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala is a religious painting created to depict sacred sites, such as temples and shrines, for the purpose of promoting pilgrimage and religious devotion. Many surviving examples were painted on paper and used in religious fundraising campaigns.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Honjoryō|ja|寺社本所領}} Temple and Shrine Honjoryō refers to the estates belonging to religious institutions in contrast to those controlled by the warrior class. Before the 12th century, there was no clear distinction, but as the Kamakura shogunate strengthened its authority, temple and shrine lands were increasingly categorized separately from warrior-held territories.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Regulations|ja|寺社法度}} Temple Regulations were a set of legal codes used by the Edo shogunate and local lords to govern religious institutions. These laws often granted temple and shrine lands while imposing various controls, such as restrictions on logging within temple and shrine precincts.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|ja|寺社興行法}} The Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws refer to legal measures implemented by secular authorities to restore and regulate religious ceremonies. These laws aimed to revive religious practices that had declined due to various circumstances, particularly from the late Heian period onward.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Law|ja|寺社法}} Temple Law, also known as Temple and Shrine Law, refers to the legal framework governing religious institutions in premodern Japan. This framework involved two main types of authority: secular control over religious institutions and self-regulation by the religious communities themselves.
== See Also == * {{ill|Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} * {{ill|Temple and Shrine Authorities|ja|寺社勢力}} * {{ill|Magistrates for Temples and Shrines|ja|寺社奉行}} * {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} * {{ill|List of Temples in Japan|ja|日本の寺院一覧}} * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Japan|ja|神社一覧}}

revision.text:
{{Original research|date=January 2011}} '''Temples and Shrines''' (寺社, Jisha) is a collective term in Japan referring to both {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}} and {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}. It is also called '''Shrines and Temples''' (社寺, Shaji) or '''Shrines and Temple Precincts''' (神社仏閣, Jinja Bukkaku).
The term "Jisha" reflects the idea of Buddhist supremacy over Shinto that prevailed until the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. On the other hand, the term "Shaji" appeared as early as the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, primarily reflecting a preference for shrines and was widely used after the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}.
== Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism ==
Both temples and shrines (and their deities, {{ill|kami|ja|神}} and {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏}}) have been objects of faith in Japan, and prior to the {{ill|separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離}} during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, they were often treated collectively without distinction when not necessary.
The history of {{ill|syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} lies in the blending of native {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} beliefs and imported {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}} (especially {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}), which gave rise to diverse faiths like {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道}}. Theoretical frameworks like the {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹}} doctrine attempted to unify these practices, with "Gongen" (権現) serving as an ambiguous term representing both. Examples of architectural integration include "Jingūji" (神宮寺, Shrine-Temples).
During the Meiji period's separation policies, syncretized entities were forcibly divided into either shrines or temples, often resulting in the abolition of one side.
However, at the grassroots level, these changes caused minimal shifts in popular consciousness. Local {{ill|folk religion|lt=folk religions|ja|民間信仰}} and {{ill|festivals|ja|祭り}} continued relatively unchanged, preserving traditions beyond the scope of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}}.
In modern times, the differences between temples and shrines are seldom emphasized outside specific rituals like funerals. Japanese people are often described as having a "weak sense of faith," with temples and shrines viewed more for their {{ill|worldly benefits|ja|現世利益}} or as {{ill|tourist destinations|ja|観光地}}.
==Articles with this term in their title== {{ill|Temple and Shrine Estates|ja|寺社領}} Temple and Shrine Estates (じしゃりょう) refer to one of the territorial divisions that existed in Japan in the past. Primarily, these estates were established to support the maintenance and operation of temples and shrines. In ancient times, revenue sources for temples and shrines included self-owned lands (shinden and teraden), grants from the ritsuryō state, and early shōen estates developed through reclamation efforts. Some major temples and shrines held extensive territories that provided significant income.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Magistrate|ja|寺社奉行}} The Temple and Shrine Magistrate (じしゃぶぎょう) was an official position within the military governments from the Muromachi period through the Edo period, responsible for overseeing religious administration. Originating in the Kamakura shogunate, this office managed temple and shrine estates, buildings, monks, and shrine priests. The Edo shogunate established this position in 1635 (Kan'ei 12), placing it under the direct authority of the shogun and ranking it highest among the three magistrates. The magistrate also oversaw other cultural and administrative aspects related to religious institutions.
{{ill|Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line|ja|新潟県道590号保田寺社線}} Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line (にいがたけんどう590ごう やすだじしゃせん) is a general prefectural road in Agano City, Niigata Prefecture. It serves as an access route from National Route 49 to the Niigata Eastern Industrial Complex. - Starting point: Yasuda, Agano City, Niigata Prefecture (intersection with Niigata Prefectural Route 55) - Endpoint: Jisha, Agano City (intersection with National Route 49)
{{ill|Miyadaiku|ja|宮大工}} (redirect from {{ill|Temple and Shrine Carpenter|ja|寺社大工}}) Miyadaiku refers to master carpenters specializing in the construction and restoration of temples and shrines. These craftsmen maintain unique traditional techniques, including the use of yari kanna (a spear-plane), which has largely disappeared from general carpentry. Only a handful of specialist companies, such as Takigawa Jisha Architecture in Nara, are entrusted with the restoration of National Treasure and Important Cultural Property buildings.
{{ill|Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records|ja|大乗院寺社雑事記}} The Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records (だいじょういんじしゃぞうじき) is a diary written over three generations by Jinson, Shōkaku, and Kyōjin, monks who managed Kōfuku-ji’s Daijōin in the Muromachi period. Spanning approximately 190 volumes, the surviving records from 1450 (Hōtoku 2) to 1527 (Daiei 7) are preserved in the National Archives of Japan and designated as Important Cultural Property.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Power|ja|寺社勢力}} The Temple and Shrine Power (じしゃせいりょく) refers to the military, administrative, economic, and cultural influence of large temples and shrines in medieval Japan. Alongside the military government and the imperial court, religious institutions played a central role in politics and governance. Due to the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, many major religious sites wielded substantial territorial control and maintained private armies.
{{ill|Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|ja|京都寺社等同時放火事件}} The Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident was a series of arson attacks that took place in Kyoto on April 24-25, 1993. A senior member of the Japanese New Leftist group Chūkakuha was arrested and charged as the suspected perpetrator, but was later acquitted, with the ruling becoming final.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala|ja|社寺参詣曼荼羅}} A Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala is a religious painting created to depict sacred sites, such as temples and shrines, for the purpose of promoting pilgrimage and religious devotion. Many surviving examples were painted on paper and used in religious fundraising campaigns.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Honjoryō|ja|寺社本所領}} Temple and Shrine Honjoryō refers to the estates belonging to religious institutions in contrast to those controlled by the warrior class. Before the 12th century, there was no clear distinction, but as the Kamakura shogunate strengthened its authority, temple and shrine lands were increasingly categorized separately from warrior-held territories.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Regulations|ja|寺社法度}} Temple Regulations were a set of legal codes used by the Edo shogunate and local lords to govern religious institutions. These laws often granted temple and shrine lands while imposing various controls, such as restrictions on logging within temple and shrine precincts.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|ja|寺社興行法}} The Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws refer to legal measures implemented by secular authorities to restore and regulate religious ceremonies. These laws aimed to revive religious practices that had declined due to various circumstances, particularly from the late Heian period onward.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Law|ja|寺社法}} Temple Law, also known as Temple and Shrine Law, refers to the legal framework governing religious institutions in premodern Japan. This framework involved two main types of authority: secular control over religious institutions and self-regulation by the religious communities themselves.== See Also == * {{ill|Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} * {{ill|Temple and Shrine Authorities|ja|寺社勢力}} * {{ill|Magistrates for Temples and Shrines|ja|寺社奉行}} * {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} * {{ill|List of Temples in Japan|ja|日本の寺院一覧}} * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Japan|ja|神社一覧}}

{{Original research|date=January 2011}} '''Temples and Shrines''' (寺社, Jisha) is a collective term in Japan referring to both {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}} and {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}. It is also called '''Shrines and Temples''' (社寺, Shaji) or '''Shrines and Temple Precincts''' (神社仏閣, Jinja Bukkaku).
The term "Jisha" reflects the idea of Buddhist supremacy over Shinto that prevailed until the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. On the other hand, the term "Shaji" appeared as early as the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, primarily reflecting a preference for shrines and was widely used after the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}.
== Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism ==
Both temples and shrines (and their deities, {{ill|kami|ja|神}} and {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏}}) have been objects of faith in Japan, and prior to the {{ill|separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離}} during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, they were often treated collectively without distinction when not necessary.
The history of {{ill|syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} lies in the blending of native {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} beliefs and imported {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}} (especially {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}), which gave rise to diverse faiths like {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道}}. Theoretical frameworks like the {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹}} doctrine attempted to unify these practices, with "Gongen" (権現) serving as an ambiguous term representing both. Examples of architectural integration include "Jingūji" (神宮寺, Shrine-Temples).
During the Meiji period's separation policies, syncretized entities were forcibly divided into either shrines or temples, often resulting in the abolition of one side.
However, at the grassroots level, these changes caused minimal shifts in popular consciousness. Local {{ill|folk religion|lt=folk religions|ja|民間信仰}} and {{ill|festivals|ja|祭り}} continued relatively unchanged, preserving traditions beyond the scope of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}}.
In modern times, the differences between temples and shrines are seldom emphasized outside specific rituals like funerals. Japanese people are often described as having a "weak sense of faith," with temples and shrines viewed more for their {{ill|worldly benefits|ja|現世利益}} or as {{ill|tourist destinations|ja|観光地}}.
==Articles with this term in their title== {{ill|Temple and Shrine Estates|ja|寺社領}} Temple and Shrine Estates (じしゃりょう) refer to one of the territorial divisions that existed in Japan in the past. Primarily, these estates were established to support the maintenance and operation of temples and shrines. In ancient times, revenue sources for temples and shrines included self-owned lands (shinden and teraden), grants from the ritsuryō state, and early shōen estates developed through reclamation efforts. Some major temples and shrines held extensive territories that provided significant income.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Magistrate|ja|寺社奉行}} The Temple and Shrine Magistrate (じしゃぶぎょう) was an official position within the military governments from the Muromachi period through the Edo period, responsible for overseeing religious administration. Originating in the Kamakura shogunate, this office managed temple and shrine estates, buildings, monks, and shrine priests. The Edo shogunate established this position in 1635 (Kan'ei 12), placing it under the direct authority of the shogun and ranking it highest among the three magistrates. The magistrate also oversaw other cultural and administrative aspects related to religious institutions.
{{ill|Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line|ja|新潟県道590号保田寺社線}} Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line (にいがたけんどう590ごう やすだじしゃせん) is a general prefectural road in Agano City, Niigata Prefecture. It serves as an access route from National Route 49 to the Niigata Eastern Industrial Complex. - Starting point: Yasuda, Agano City, Niigata Prefecture (intersection with Niigata Prefectural Route 55) - Endpoint: Jisha, Agano City (intersection with National Route 49)
{{ill|Miyadaiku|ja|宮大工}} (redirect from {{ill|Temple and Shrine Carpenter|ja|寺社大工}}) Miyadaiku refers to master carpenters specializing in the construction and restoration of temples and shrines. These craftsmen maintain unique traditional techniques, including the use of yari kanna (a spear-plane), which has largely disappeared from general carpentry. Only a handful of specialist companies, such as Takigawa Jisha Architecture in Nara, are entrusted with the restoration of National Treasure and Important Cultural Property buildings.
{{ill|Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records|ja|大乗院寺社雑事記}} The Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records (だいじょういんじしゃぞうじき) is a diary written over three generations by Jinson, Shōkaku, and Kyōjin, monks who managed Kōfuku-ji’s Daijōin in the Muromachi period. Spanning approximately 190 volumes, the surviving records from 1450 (Hōtoku 2) to 1527 (Daiei 7) are preserved in the National Archives of Japan and designated as Important Cultural Property.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Power|ja|寺社勢力}} The Temple and Shrine Power (じしゃせいりょく) refers to the military, administrative, economic, and cultural influence of large temples and shrines in medieval Japan. Alongside the military government and the imperial court, religious institutions played a central role in politics and governance. Due to the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, many major religious sites wielded substantial territorial control and maintained private armies.
{{ill|Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|ja|京都寺社等同時放火事件}} The Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident was a series of arson attacks that took place in Kyoto on April 24-25, 1993. A senior member of the Japanese New Leftist group Chūkakuha was arrested and charged as the suspected perpetrator, but was later acquitted, with the ruling becoming final.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala|ja|社寺参詣曼荼羅}} A Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala is a religious painting created to depict sacred sites, such as temples and shrines, for the purpose of promoting pilgrimage and religious devotion. Many surviving examples were painted on paper and used in religious fundraising campaigns.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Honjoryō|ja|寺社本所領}} Temple and Shrine Honjoryō refers to the estates belonging to religious institutions in contrast to those controlled by the warrior class. Before the 12th century, there was no clear distinction, but as the Kamakura shogunate strengthened its authority, temple and shrine lands were increasingly categorized separately from warrior-held territories.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Regulations|ja|寺社法度}} Temple Regulations were a set of legal codes used by the Edo shogunate and local lords to govern religious institutions. These laws often granted temple and shrine lands while imposing various controls, such as restrictions on logging within temple and shrine precincts.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|ja|寺社興行法}} The Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws refer to legal measures implemented by secular authorities to restore and regulate religious ceremonies. These laws aimed to revive religious practices that had declined due to various circumstances, particularly from the late Heian period onward.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Law|ja|寺社法}} Temple Law, also known as Temple and Shrine Law, refers to the legal framework governing religious institutions in premodern Japan. This framework involved two main types of authority: secular control over religious institutions and self-regulation by the religious communities themselves.== See Also == * {{ill|Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} * {{ill|Temple and Shrine Authorities|ja|寺社勢力}} * {{ill|Magistrates for Temples and Shrines|ja|寺社奉行}} * {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} * {{ill|List of Temples in Japan|ja|日本の寺院一覧}} * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Japan|ja|神社一覧}}

revision.text:
{{Original research|date=January 2011}} '''Temples and Shrines''' (寺社, Jisha) is a collective term in Japan referring to both {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}} and {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}. It is also called '''Shrines and Temples''' (社寺, Shaji) or '''Shrines and Temple Precincts''' (神社仏閣, Jinja Bukkaku).
The term "Jisha" reflects the idea of Buddhist supremacy over Shinto that prevailed until the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. On the other hand, the term "Shaji" appeared as early as the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, primarily reflecting a preference for shrines and was widely used after the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}.
== Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism ==
Both temples and shrines (and their deities, {{ill|kami|ja|神}} and {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏}}) have been objects of faith in Japan, and prior to the {{ill|separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離}} during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, they were often treated collectively without distinction when not necessary.
The history of {{ill|syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} lies in the blending of native {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} beliefs and imported {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}} (especially {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}), which gave rise to diverse faiths like {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道}}. Theoretical frameworks like the {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹}} doctrine attempted to unify these practices, with "Gongen" (権現) serving as an ambiguous term representing both. Examples of architectural integration include "Jingūji" (神宮寺, Shrine-Temples).
During the Meiji period's separation policies, syncretized entities were forcibly divided into either shrines or temples, often resulting in the abolition of one side.
However, at the grassroots level, these changes caused minimal shifts in popular consciousness. Local {{ill|folk religion|lt=folk religions|ja|民間信仰}} and {{ill|festivals|ja|祭り}} continued relatively unchanged, preserving traditions beyond the scope of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}}.
In modern times, the differences between temples and shrines are seldom emphasized outside specific rituals like funerals. Japanese people are often described as having a "weak sense of faith," with temples and shrines viewed more for their {{ill|worldly benefits|ja|現世利益}} or as {{ill|tourist destinations|ja|観光地}}.
==Articles with this term in their title== {{ill|Temple and Shrine Estates|ja|寺社領}} Temple and Shrine Estates (じしゃりょう) refer to one of the territorial divisions that existed in Japan in the past. Primarily, these estates were established to support the maintenance and operation of temples and shrines. In ancient times, revenue sources for temples and shrines included self-owned lands (shinden and teraden), grants from the ritsuryō state, and early shōen estates developed through reclamation efforts. Some major temples and shrines held extensive territories that provided significant income.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Magistrate|ja|寺社奉行}} The Temple and Shrine Magistrate (じしゃぶぎょう) was an official position within the military governments from the Muromachi period through the Edo period, responsible for overseeing religious administration. Originating in the Kamakura shogunate, this office managed temple and shrine estates, buildings, monks, and shrine priests. The Edo shogunate established this position in 1635 (Kan'ei 12), placing it under the direct authority of the shogun and ranking it highest among the three magistrates. The magistrate also oversaw other cultural and administrative aspects related to religious institutions.
{{ill|Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line|ja|新潟県道590号保田寺社線}} Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line (にいがたけんどう590ごう やすだじしゃせん) is a general prefectural road in Agano City, Niigata Prefecture. It serves as an access route from National Route 49 to the Niigata Eastern Industrial Complex. - Starting point: Yasuda, Agano City, Niigata Prefecture (intersection with Niigata Prefectural Route 55) - Endpoint: Jisha, Agano City (intersection with National Route 49)
{{ill|Miyadaiku|ja|宮大工}} (redirect from {{ill|Temple and Shrine Carpenter|ja|寺社大工}}) Miyadaiku refers to master carpenters specializing in the construction and restoration of temples and shrines. These craftsmen maintain unique traditional techniques, including the use of yari kanna (a spear-plane), which has largely disappeared from general carpentry. Only a handful of specialist companies, such as Takigawa Jisha Architecture in Nara, are entrusted with the restoration of National Treasure and Important Cultural Property buildings.
{{ill|Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records|ja|大乗院寺社雑事記}} The Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records (だいじょういんじしゃぞうじき) is a diary written over three generations by Jinson, Shōkaku, and Kyōjin, monks who managed Kōfuku-ji’s Daijōin in the Muromachi period. Spanning approximately 190 volumes, the surviving records from 1450 (Hōtoku 2) to 1527 (Daiei 7) are preserved in the National Archives of Japan and designated as Important Cultural Property.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Power|ja|寺社勢力}} The Temple and Shrine Power (じしゃせいりょく) refers to the military, administrative, economic, and cultural influence of large temples and shrines in medieval Japan. Alongside the military government and the imperial court, religious institutions played a central role in politics and governance. Due to the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, many major religious sites wielded substantial territorial control and maintained private armies.
{{ill|Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|ja|京都寺社等同時放火事件}} The Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident was a series of arson attacks that took place in Kyoto on April 24-25, 1993. A senior member of the Japanese New Leftist group Chūkakuha was arrested and charged as the suspected perpetrator, but was later acquitted, with the ruling becoming final.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala|ja|社寺参詣曼荼羅}} A Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala is a religious painting created to depict sacred sites, such as temples and shrines, for the purpose of promoting pilgrimage and religious devotion. Many surviving examples were painted on paper and used in religious fundraising campaigns.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Honjoryō|ja|寺社本所領}} Temple and Shrine Honjoryō refers to the estates belonging to religious institutions in contrast to those controlled by the warrior class. Before the 12th century, there was no clear distinction, but as the Kamakura shogunate strengthened its authority, temple and shrine lands were increasingly categorized separately from warrior-held territories.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Regulations|ja|寺社法度}} Temple Regulations were a set of legal codes used by the Edo shogunate and local lords to govern religious institutions. These laws often granted temple and shrine lands while imposing various controls, such as restrictions on logging within temple and shrine precincts.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|ja|寺社興行法}} The Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws refer to legal measures implemented by secular authorities to restore and regulate religious ceremonies. These laws aimed to revive religious practices that had declined due to various circumstances, particularly from the late Heian period onward.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Law|ja|寺社法}} Temple Law, also known as Temple and Shrine Law, refers to the legal framework governing religious institutions in premodern Japan. This framework involved two main types of authority: secular control over religious institutions and self-regulation by the religious communities themselves. == See Also! == * {{ill|Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} * {{ill|Temple and Shrine Authorities|ja|寺社勢力}} * {{ill|Magistrates for Temples and Shrines|ja|寺社奉行}} * {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} * {{ill|List of Temples in Japan|ja|日本の寺院一覧}} * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Japan|ja|神社一覧}}

revision.text:
{{Original research|date=January 2011}} '''Temples and Shrines''' (寺社, Jisha) is a collective term in Japan referring to both {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}} and {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}. It is also called '''Shrines and Temples''' (社寺, Shaji) or '''Shrines and Temple Precincts''' (神社仏閣, Jinja Bukkaku).
The term "Jisha" reflects the idea of Buddhist supremacy over Shinto that prevailed until the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. On the other hand, the term "Shaji" appeared as early as the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, primarily reflecting a preference for shrines and was widely used after the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}.
== Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism ==
Both temples and shrines (and their deities, {{ill|kami|ja|神}} and {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏}}) have been objects of faith in Japan, and prior to the {{ill|separation of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏分離}} during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, they were often treated collectively without distinction when not necessary.
The history of {{ill|syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} lies in the blending of native {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} beliefs and imported {{ill|Buddhism|ja|仏教}} (especially {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}}), which gave rise to diverse faiths like {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道}}. Theoretical frameworks like the {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹}} doctrine attempted to unify these practices, with "Gongen" (権現) serving as an ambiguous term representing both. Examples of architectural integration include "Jingūji" (神宮寺, Shrine-Temples).
During the Meiji period's separation policies, syncretized entities were forcibly divided into either shrines or temples, often resulting in the abolition of one side.
However, at the grassroots level, these changes caused minimal shifts in popular consciousness. Local {{ill|folk religion|lt=folk religions|ja|民間信仰}} and {{ill|festivals|ja|祭り}} continued relatively unchanged, preserving traditions beyond the scope of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}}.
In modern times, the differences between temples and shrines are seldom emphasized outside specific rituals like funerals. Japanese people are often described as having a "weak sense of faith," with temples and shrines viewed more for their {{ill|worldly benefits|ja|現世利益}} or as {{ill|tourist destinations|ja|観光地}}.
==Articles with this term in their title== {{ill|Temple and Shrine Estates|ja|寺社領}} Temple and Shrine Estates (じしゃりょう) refer to one of the territorial divisions that existed in Japan in the past. Primarily, these estates were established to support the maintenance and operation of temples and shrines. In ancient times, revenue sources for temples and shrines included self-owned lands (shinden and teraden), grants from the ritsuryō state, and early shōen estates developed through reclamation efforts. Some major temples and shrines held extensive territories that provided significant income.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Magistrate|ja|寺社奉行}} The Temple and Shrine Magistrate (じしゃぶぎょう) was an official position within the military governments from the Muromachi period through the Edo period, responsible for overseeing religious administration. Originating in the Kamakura shogunate, this office managed temple and shrine estates, buildings, monks, and shrine priests. The Edo shogunate established this position in 1635 (Kan'ei 12), placing it under the direct authority of the shogun and ranking it highest among the three magistrates. The magistrate also oversaw other cultural and administrative aspects related to religious institutions.
{{ill|Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line|ja|新潟県道590号保田寺社線}} Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line (にいがたけんどう590ごう やすだじしゃせん) is a general prefectural road in Agano City, Niigata Prefecture. It serves as an access route from National Route 49 to the Niigata Eastern Industrial Complex. - Starting point: Yasuda, Agano City, Niigata Prefecture (intersection with Niigata Prefectural Route 55) - Endpoint: Jisha, Agano City (intersection with National Route 49)
{{ill|Miyadaiku|ja|宮大工}} (redirect from {{ill|Temple and Shrine Carpenter|ja|寺社大工}}) Miyadaiku refers to master carpenters specializing in the construction and restoration of temples and shrines. These craftsmen maintain unique traditional techniques, including the use of yari kanna (a spear-plane), which has largely disappeared from general carpentry. Only a handful of specialist companies, such as Takigawa Jisha Architecture in Nara, are entrusted with the restoration of National Treasure and Important Cultural Property buildings.
{{ill|Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records|ja|大乗院寺社雑事記}} The Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records (だいじょういんじしゃぞうじき) is a diary written over three generations by Jinson, Shōkaku, and Kyōjin, monks who managed Kōfuku-ji’s Daijōin in the Muromachi period. Spanning approximately 190 volumes, the surviving records from 1450 (Hōtoku 2) to 1527 (Daiei 7) are preserved in the National Archives of Japan and designated as Important Cultural Property.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Power|ja|寺社勢力}} The Temple and Shrine Power (じしゃせいりょく) refers to the military, administrative, economic, and cultural influence of large temples and shrines in medieval Japan. Alongside the military government and the imperial court, religious institutions played a central role in politics and governance. Due to the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, many major religious sites wielded substantial territorial control and maintained private armies.
{{ill|Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|ja|京都寺社等同時放火事件}} The Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident was a series of arson attacks that took place in Kyoto on April 24-25, 1993. A senior member of the Japanese New Leftist group Chūkakuha was arrested and charged as the suspected perpetrator, but was later acquitted, with the ruling becoming final.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala|ja|社寺参詣曼荼羅}} A Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala is a religious painting created to depict sacred sites, such as temples and shrines, for the purpose of promoting pilgrimage and religious devotion. Many surviving examples were painted on paper and used in religious fundraising campaigns.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Honjoryō|ja|寺社本所領}} Temple and Shrine Honjoryō refers to the estates belonging to religious institutions in contrast to those controlled by the warrior class. Before the 12th century, there was no clear distinction, but as the Kamakura shogunate strengthened its authority, temple and shrine lands were increasingly categorized separately from warrior-held territories.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Regulations|ja|寺社法度}} Temple Regulations were a set of legal codes used by the Edo shogunate and local lords to govern religious institutions. These laws often granted temple and shrine lands while imposing various controls, such as restrictions on logging within temple and shrine precincts.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|ja|寺社興行法}} The Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws refer to legal measures implemented by secular authorities to restore and regulate religious ceremonies. These laws aimed to revive religious practices that had declined due to various circumstances, particularly from the late Heian period onward.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Law|ja|寺社法}} Temple Law, also known as Temple and Shrine Law, refers to the legal framework governing religious institutions in premodern Japan. This framework involved two main types of authority: secular control over religious institutions and self-regulation by the religious communities themselves. == See Also == * {{ill|Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} * {{ill|Temple and Shrine Authorities|ja|寺社勢力}} * {{ill|Magistrates for Temples and Shrines|ja|寺社奉行}} * {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} * {{ill|List of Temples in Japan|ja|日本の寺院一覧}} * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Japan|ja|神社一覧}}
