Spanning approximately 190 volumes, the surviving records from 1450 (Hōtoku 2) to 1527 (Daiei 7) are preserved in the National Archives of Japan and designated as Important Cultural Property.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Power|ja|寺社勢力|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Power|qq=}} The Temple and Shrine Power (じしゃせいりょく) refers to the military, administrative, economic, and cultural influence of large temples and shrines in medieval Japan. Alongside the military government and the imperial court, religious institutions played a central role in politics and governance. Due to the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, many major religious sites wielded substantial territorial control and maintained private armies.
{{ill|Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|ja|京都寺社等同時放火事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|1=Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11374949}} The Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident was a series of arson attacks that took place in Kyoto on April 24-25, 1993. A senior member of the Japanese New Leftist group Chūkakuha was arrested and charged as the suspected perpetrator, but was later acquitted, with the ruling becoming final.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala|ja|社寺参詣曼荼羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala|qq=}} A Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala is a religious painting created to depict sacred sites, such as temples and shrines, for the purpose of promoting pilgrimage and religious devotion. Many surviving examples were painted on paper and used in religious fundraising campaigns.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Honjoryō|ja|寺社本所領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Honjoryō|1=jisha-honjo-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458054}} Temple and Shrine Honjoryō refers to the estates belonging to religious institutions in contrast to those controlled by the warrior class. Before the 12th century, there was no clear distinction, but as the Kamakura shogunate strengthened its authority, temple and shrine lands were increasingly categorized separately from warrior-held territories.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Regulations|ja|寺社法度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Regulations|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺院諸法度|1=Jiin shohatto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5361074}} Temple Regulations were a set of legal codes used by the Edo shogunate and local lords to govern religious institutions. These laws often granted temple and shrine lands while imposing various controls, such as restrictions on logging within temple and shrine precincts.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|ja|寺社興行法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|1=Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458055}} The Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws refer to legal measures implemented by secular authorities to restore and regulate religious ceremonies. These laws aimed to revive religious practices that had declined due to various circumstances, particularly from the late Heian period onward.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Law|ja|寺社法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Law|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺院法}} Temple Law, also known as Temple and Shrine Law, refers to the legal framework governing religious institutions in premodern Japan. This framework involved two main types of authority: secular control over religious institutions and self-regulation by the religious communities themselves. == See Also == * [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] * {{ill|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Authorities|ja|寺社勢力|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Authorities|qq=}} * [[:en:Jisha-bugyō|Magistrates for Temples and Shrines]] * [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] * [[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Japan|List of Temples in Japan]] * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Japan|List of Shinto shrines in Japan]]

{{ill|Temple and Shrine Honjoryō|ja|寺社本所領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Honjoryō|1=jisha-honjo-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458054}} Temple and Shrine Honjoryō refers to the estates belonging to religious institutions in contrast to those controlled by the warrior class. Before the 12th century, there was no clear distinction, but as the Kamakura shogunate strengthened its authority, temple and shrine lands were increasingly categorized separately from warrior-held territories.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Regulations|ja|寺社法度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Regulations|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺院諸法度|1=Jiin shohatto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5361074}} Temple Regulations were a set of legal codes used by the Edo shogunate and local lords to govern religious institutions. These laws often granted temple and shrine lands while imposing various controls, such as restrictions on logging within temple and shrine precincts.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|ja|寺社興行法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|1=Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458055}} The Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws refer to legal measures implemented by secular authorities to restore and regulate religious ceremonies. These laws aimed to revive religious practices that had declined due to various circumstances, particularly from the late Heian period onward.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Law|ja|寺社法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Law|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺院法}} Temple Law, also known as Temple and Shrine Law, refers to the legal framework governing religious institutions in premodern Japan. This framework involved two main types of authority: secular control over religious institutions and self-regulation by the religious communities themselves. == See Also == * [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] * {{ill|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Authorities|ja|寺社勢力|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Authorities|qq=}} * [[:en:Jisha-bugyō|Magistrates for Temples and Shrines]] * [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] * [[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Japan|List of Temples in Japan]] * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Japan|List of Shinto shrines in Japan]]

<!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--> [[Category:Buddhist temples]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[zh:寺社]] {{wikidata link|Q11458051}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]
{{wikidata link|Q11458051}} [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社]][[zh:寺社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11458051]] Wikidata name: jisha [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Original research|date=January 2011}} '''Temples and Shrines''' (寺社, Jisha) is a collective term in Japan referring to both [[:en:Buddhist temple|temples]] and [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrines]]. It is also called '''Shrines and Temples''' (社寺, Shaji) or '''Shrines and Temple Precincts''' (神社仏閣, Jinja Bukkaku).
The term "Jisha" reflects the idea of Buddhist supremacy over Shinto that prevailed until the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. On the other hand, the term "Shaji" appeared as early as the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], primarily reflecting a preference for shrines and was widely used after the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]].
== Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism ==
Both temples and shrines (and their deities, [[:en:Deity|kami]] and {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhas|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仏陀|1=Buddha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7055}}) have been objects of faith in Japan, and prior to the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], they were often treated collectively without distinction when not necessary.
The history of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] lies in the blending of native [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] beliefs and imported [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] (especially [[:en:Mikkyō|Esoteric Buddhism]]), which gave rise to diverse faiths like [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]]. Theoretical frameworks like the [[:en:Honji suijaku|honji suijaku]] doctrine attempted to unify these practices, with "Gongen" (権現) serving as an ambiguous term representing both. Examples of architectural integration include "Jingūji" (神宮寺, Shrine-Temples).
During the Meiji period's separation policies, syncretized entities were forcibly divided into either shrines or temples, often resulting in the abolition of one side.
However, at the grassroots level, these changes caused minimal shifts in popular consciousness. Local [[:en:Folk religion|folk religions]] and {{ill|festivals|ja|祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/festivals|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祭|1=festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132241}} continued relatively unchanged, preserving traditions beyond the scope of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]].
In modern times, the differences between temples and shrines are seldom emphasized outside specific rituals like funerals. Japanese people are often described as having a "weak sense of faith," with temples and shrines viewed more for their {{ill|worldly benefits|ja|現世利益|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/worldly benefits|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 現世|1=this world|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20183021}} or as [[:en:Tourist attraction|tourist destinations]].
==Articles with this term in their title== [[:en:Jisha-ryō|Temple and Shrine Estates]] Temple and Shrine Estates (じしゃりょう) refer to one of the territorial divisions that existed in Japan in the past. Primarily, these estates were established to support the maintenance and operation of temples and shrines. In ancient times, revenue sources for temples and shrines included self-owned lands (shinden and teraden), grants from the ritsuryō state, and early shōen estates developed through reclamation efforts. Some major temples and shrines held extensive territories that provided significant income.
[[:en:Jisha-bugyō|Temple and Shrine Magistrate]] The Temple and Shrine Magistrate (じしゃぶぎょう) was an official position within the military governments from the Muromachi period through the Edo period, responsible for overseeing religious administration. Originating in the Kamakura shogunate, this office managed temple and shrine estates, buildings, monks, and shrine priests. The Edo shogunate established this position in 1635 (Kan'ei 12), placing it under the direct authority of the shogun and Ranking it highest among the three magistrates. The magistrate also oversaw other cultural and administrative aspects related to religious institutions.
{{ill|Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line|ja|新潟県道590号保田寺社線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line|1=Niigata Prefectural Road Route 590|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16484359}} Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line (にいがたけんどう590ごう やすだじしゃせん) is a general prefectural road in Agano City, Niigata Prefecture. It serves as an access route from National Route 49 to the Niigata Eastern Industrial Complex. - Starting point: Yasuda, Agano City, Niigata Prefecture (intersection with Niigata Prefectural Route 55) - Endpoint: Jisha, Agano City (intersection with National Route 49)
{{ill|Miyadaiku|ja|宮大工|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyadaiku|1=miyadaiku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65261787}} (redirect from {{ill|Temple and Shrine Carpenter|ja|寺社大工|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Carpenter|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮大工|1=miyadaiku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65261787}}) Miyadaiku refers to master carpenters specializing in the construction and restoration of temples and shrines. These craftsmen maintain unique traditional techniques, including the use of yari kanna (a spear-plane), which has largely disappeared from general carpentry. Only a handful of specialist companies, such as Takigawa Jisha Architecture in Nara, are entrusted with the restoration of National Treasure and Important Cultural Property buildings.
{{ill|Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records|ja|大乗院寺社雑事記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records|1=Daijōin Jisha Zōjiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432101}} The Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records (だいじょういんじしゃぞうじき) is a diary written over three generations by Jinson, Shōkaku, and Kyōjin, monks who managed Kōfuku-ji’s Daijōin in the Muromachi period. Spanning approximately 190 volumes, the surviving records from 1450 (Hōtoku 2) to 1527 (Daiei 7) are preserved in the National Archives of Japan and designated as Important Cultural Property.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Power|ja|寺社勢力|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Power|qq=}} The Temple and Shrine Power (じしゃせいりょく) refers to the military, administrative, economic, and cultural influence of large temples and shrines in medieval Japan. Alongside the military government and the imperial court, religious institutions played a central role in politics and governance. Due to the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, many major religious sites wielded substantial territorial control and maintained private armies.
{{ill|Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|ja|京都寺社等同時放火事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|1=Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11374949}} The Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident was a series of arson attacks that took place in Kyoto on April 24-25, 1993. A senior member of the Japanese New Leftist group Chūkakuha was arrested and charged as the suspected perpetrator, but was later acquitted, with the ruling becoming final.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala|ja|社寺参詣曼荼羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala|qq=}} A Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala is a religious painting created to depict sacred sites, such as temples and shrines, for the purpose of promoting pilgrimage and religious devotion. Many surviving examples were painted on paper and used in religious fundraising campaigns.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Honjoryō|ja|寺社本所領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Honjoryō|1=jisha-honjo-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458054}} Temple and Shrine Honjoryō refers to the estates belonging to religious institutions in contrast to those controlled by the warrior class. Before the 12th century, there was no clear distinction, but as the Kamakura shogunate strengthened its authority, temple and shrine lands were increasingly categorized separately from warrior-held territories.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Regulations|ja|寺社法度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Regulations|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺院諸法度|1=Jiin shohatto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5361074}} Temple Regulations were a set of legal codes used by the Edo shogunate and local lords to govern religious institutions. These laws often granted temple and shrine lands while imposing various controls, such as restrictions on logging within temple and shrine precincts.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|ja|寺社興行法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|1=Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458055}} The Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws refer to legal measures implemented by secular authorities to restore and regulate religious ceremonies. These laws aimed to revive religious practices that had declined due to various circumstances, particularly from the late Heian period onward.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Law|ja|寺社法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Law|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺院法}} Temple Law, also known as Temple and Shrine Law, refers to the legal framework governing religious institutions in premodern Japan. This framework involved two main types of authority: secular control over religious institutions and self-regulation by the religious communities themselves. == See Also == * [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] * {{ill|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Authorities|ja|寺社勢力|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Authorities|qq=}} * [[:en:Jisha-bugyō|Magistrates for Temples and Shrines]] * [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] * [[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Japan|List of Temples in Japan]] * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Japan|List of Shinto shrines in Japan]]
{{Reli-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|寺社|version=87669248|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社]][[zh:寺社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社]][[zh:寺社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11458051]] Wikidata name: jisha [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Original research|date=January 2011}} '''Temples and Shrines''' (寺社, Jisha) is a collective term in Japan referring to both [[:en:Buddhist temple|temples]] and [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrines]]. It is also called '''Shrines and Temples''' (社寺, Shaji) or '''Shrines and Temple Precincts''' (神社仏閣, Jinja Bukkaku).

On the other hand, the term "Shaji" appeared as early as the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], primarily reflecting a preference for shrines and was widely used after the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]].
== Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism ==
Both temples and shrines (and their deities, [[:en:Deity|kami]] and {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhas|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仏陀|1=Buddha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7055}}) have been objects of faith in Japan, and prior to the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], they were often treated collectively without distinction when not necessary.
The history of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] lies in the blending of native [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] beliefs and imported [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] (especially [[:en:Mikkyō|Esoteric Buddhism]]), which gave rise to diverse faiths like [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]]. Theoretical frameworks like the [[:en:Honji suijaku|honji suijaku]] doctrine attempted to unify these practices, with "Gongen" (権現) serving as an ambiguous term representing both. Examples of architectural integration include "Jingūji" (神宮寺, Shrine-Temples).
During the Meiji period's separation policies, syncretized entities were forcibly divided into either shrines or temples, often resulting in the abolition of one side.
However, at the grassroots level, these changes caused minimal shifts in popular consciousness. Local [[:en:Folk religion|folk religions]] and {{ill|festivals|ja|祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/festivals|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祭|1=festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132241}} continued relatively unchanged, preserving traditions beyond the scope of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]].
In modern times, the differences between temples and shrines are seldom emphasized outside specific rituals like funerals. Japanese people are often described as having a "weak sense of faith," with temples and shrines viewed more for their {{ill|worldly benefits|ja|現世利益|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/worldly benefits|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 現世|1=this world|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20183021}} or as [[:en:Tourist attraction|tourist destinations]].
==Articles with this term in their title== [[:en:Jisha-ryō|Temple and Shrine Estates]] Temple and Shrine Estates (じしゃりょう) refer to one of the territorial divisions that existed in Japan in the past. Primarily, these estates were established to support the maintenance and operation of temples and shrines. In ancient times, revenue sources for temples and shrines included self-owned lands (shinden and teraden), grants from the ritsuryō state, and early shōen estates developed through reclamation efforts. Some major temples and shrines held extensive territories that provided significant income.
[[:en:Jisha-bugyō|Temple and Shrine Magistrate]] The Temple and Shrine Magistrate (じしゃぶぎょう) was an official position within the military governments from the Muromachi period through the Edo period, responsible for overseeing religious administration. Originating in the Kamakura shogunate, this office managed temple and shrine estates, buildings, monks, and shrine priests. The Edo shogunate established this position in 1635 (Kan'ei 12), placing it under the direct authority of the shogun and Ranking it highest among the three magistrates. The magistrate also oversaw other cultural and administrative aspects related to religious institutions.
{{ill|Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line|ja|新潟県道590号保田寺社線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line|1=Niigata Prefectural Road Route 590|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16484359}} Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line (にいがたけんどう590ごう やすだじしゃせん) is a general prefectural road in Agano City, Niigata Prefecture. It serves as an access route from National Route 49 to the Niigata Eastern Industrial Complex. - Starting point: Yasuda, Agano City, Niigata Prefecture (intersection with Niigata Prefectural Route 55) - Endpoint: Jisha, Agano City (intersection with National Route 49)
{{ill|Miyadaiku|ja|宮大工|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyadaiku|1=miyadaiku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65261787}} (redirect from {{ill|Temple and Shrine Carpenter|ja|寺社大工|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Carpenter|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮大工|1=miyadaiku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65261787}}) Miyadaiku refers to master carpenters specializing in the construction and restoration of temples and shrines. These craftsmen maintain unique traditional techniques, including the use of yari kanna (a spear-plane), which has largely disappeared from general carpentry. Only a handful of specialist companies, such as Takigawa Jisha Architecture in Nara, are entrusted with the restoration of National Treasure and Important Cultural Property buildings.
{{ill|Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records|ja|大乗院寺社雑事記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records|1=Daijōin Jisha Zōjiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432101}} The Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records (だいじょういんじしゃぞうじき) is a diary written over three generations by Jinson, Shōkaku, and Kyōjin, monks who managed Kōfuku-ji’s Daijōin in the Muromachi period. Spanning approximately 190 volumes, the surviving records from 1450 (Hōtoku 2) to 1527 (Daiei 7) are preserved in the National Archives of Japan and designated as Important Cultural Property.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Power|ja|寺社勢力|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Power|qq=}} The Temple and Shrine Power (じしゃせいりょく) refers to the military, administrative, economic, and cultural influence of large temples and shrines in medieval Japan. Alongside the military government and the imperial court, religious institutions played a central role in politics and governance. Due to the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, many major religious sites wielded substantial territorial control and maintained private armies.
{{ill|Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|ja|京都寺社等同時放火事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|1=Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11374949}} The Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident was a series of arson attacks that took place in Kyoto on April 24-25, 1993. A senior member of the Japanese New Leftist group Chūkakuha was arrested and charged as the suspected perpetrator, but was later acquitted, with the ruling becoming final.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala|ja|社寺参詣曼荼羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala|qq=}} A Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala is a religious painting created to depict sacred sites, such as temples and shrines, for the purpose of promoting pilgrimage and religious devotion. Many surviving examples were painted on paper and used in religious fundraising campaigns.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Honjoryō|ja|寺社本所領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Honjoryō|1=jisha-honjo-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458054}} Temple and Shrine Honjoryō refers to the estates belonging to religious institutions in contrast to those controlled by the warrior class. Before the 12th century, there was no clear distinction, but as the Kamakura shogunate strengthened its authority, temple and shrine lands were increasingly categorized separately from warrior-held territories.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Regulations|ja|寺社法度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Regulations|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺院諸法度|1=Jiin shohatto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5361074}} Temple Regulations were a set of legal codes used by the Edo shogunate and local lords to govern religious institutions. These laws often granted temple and shrine lands while imposing various controls, such as restrictions on logging within temple and shrine precincts.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|ja|寺社興行法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|1=Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458055}} The Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws refer to legal measures implemented by secular authorities to restore and regulate religious ceremonies. These laws aimed to revive religious practices that had declined due to various circumstances, particularly from the late Heian period onward.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Law|ja|寺社法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Law|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺院法}} Temple Law, also known as Temple and Shrine Law, refers to the legal framework governing religious institutions in premodern Japan. This framework involved two main types of authority: secular control over religious institutions and self-regulation by the religious communities themselves. == See Also == * [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] * {{ill|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Authorities|ja|寺社勢力|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Authorities|qq=}} * [[:en:Jisha-bugyō|Magistrates for Temples and Shrines]] * [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] * [[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Japan|List of Temples in Japan]] * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Japan|List of Shinto shrines in Japan]]
{{Reli-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社]][[zh:寺社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社]][[zh:寺社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社]][[zh:寺社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11458051]] Wikidata name: jisha [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Original research|date=January 2011}} '''Temples and Shrines''' (寺社, Jisha) is a collective term in Japan referring to both [[:en:Buddhist temple|temples]] and [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrines]]. It is also called '''Shrines and Temples''' (社寺, Shaji) or '''Shrines and Temple Precincts''' (神社仏閣, Jinja Bukkaku).
The term "Jisha" reflects the idea of Buddhist supremacy over Shinto that prevailed until the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. On the other hand, the term "Shaji" appeared as early as the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], primarily reflecting a preference for shrines and was widely used after the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]].
== Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism ==
Both temples and shrines (and their deities, [[:en:Deity|kami]] and {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhas|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仏陀|1=Buddha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7055}}) have been objects of faith in Japan, and prior to the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], they were often treated collectively without distinction when not necessary.
The history of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] lies in the blending of native [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] beliefs and imported [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] (especially [[:en:Mikkyō|Esoteric Buddhism]]), which gave rise to diverse faiths like [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]]. Theoretical frameworks like the [[:en:Honji suijaku|honji suijaku]] doctrine attempted to unify these practices, with "Gongen" (権現) serving as an ambiguous term representing both. Examples of architectural integration include "Jingūji" (神宮寺, Shrine-Temples).
During the Meiji period's separation policies, syncretized entities were forcibly divided into either shrines or temples, often resulting in the abolition of one side.
However, at the grassroots level, these changes caused minimal shifts in popular consciousness. Local [[:en:Folk religion|folk religions]] and {{ill|festivals|ja|祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/festivals|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祭|1=festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132241}} continued relatively unchanged, preserving traditions beyond the scope of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]].
In modern times, the differences between temples and shrines are seldom emphasized outside specific rituals like funerals. Japanese people are often described as having a "weak sense of faith," with temples and shrines viewed more for their {{ill|worldly benefits|ja|現世利益|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/worldly benefits|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 現世|1=this world|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20183021}} or as [[:en:Tourist attraction|tourist destinations]].
==Articles with this term in their title== [[:en:Jisha-ryō|Temple and Shrine Estates]] Temple and Shrine Estates (じしゃりょう) refer to one of the territorial divisions that existed in Japan in the past. Primarily, these estates were established to support the maintenance and operation of temples and shrines. [[:en:Jisha-bugyō|Temple and Shrine Magistrate]] The Temple and Shrine Magistrate (じしゃぶぎょう) was an official position within the military governments from the Muromachi period through the Edo period, responsible for overseeing religious administration. Originating in the Kamakura shogunate, this office managed temple and shrine estates, buildings, monks, and shrine priests. The Edo shogunate established this position in 1635 (Kan'ei 12), placing it under the direct authority of the shogun and Ranking it highest among the three magistrates. The magistrate also oversaw other cultural and administrative aspects related to religious institutions.
{{ill|Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line|ja|新潟県道590号保田寺社線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line|1=Niigata Prefectural Road Route 590|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16484359}} Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line (にいがたけんどう590ごう やすだじしゃせん) is a general prefectural road in Agano City, Niigata Prefecture. It serves as an access route from National Route 49 to the Niigata Eastern Industrial Complex. - Starting point: Yasuda, Agano City, Niigata Prefecture (intersection with Niigata Prefectural Route 55) - Endpoint: Jisha, Agano City (intersection with National Route 49)
{{ill|Miyadaiku|ja|宮大工|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyadaiku|1=miyadaiku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65261787}} (redirect from {{ill|Temple and Shrine Carpenter|ja|寺社大工|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Carpenter|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮大工|1=miyadaiku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65261787}}) Miyadaiku refers to master carpenters specializing in the construction and restoration of temples and shrines. Only a handful of specialist companies, such as Takigawa Jisha Architecture in Nara, are entrusted with the restoration of National Treasure and Important Cultural Property buildings.
{{ill|Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records|ja|大乗院寺社雑事記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records|1=Daijōin Jisha Zōjiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432101}} The Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records (だいじょういんじしゃぞうじき) is a diary written over three generations by Jinson, Shōkaku, and Kyōjin, monks who managed Kōfuku-ji’s Daijōin in the Muromachi period. Spanning approximately 190 volumes, the surviving records from 1450 (Hōtoku 2) to 1527 (Daiei 7) are preserved in the National Archives of Japan and designated as Important Cultural Property.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Power|ja|寺社勢力|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Power|qq=}} The Temple and Shrine Power (じしゃせいりょく) refers to the military, administrative, economic, and cultural influence of large temples and shrines in medieval Japan. Alongside the military government and the imperial court, religious institutions played a central role in politics and governance. Due to the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, many major religious sites wielded substantial territorial control and maintained private armies.
{{ill|Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|ja|京都寺社等同時放火事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|1=Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11374949}} The Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident was a series of arson attacks that took place in Kyoto on April 24-25, 1993. A senior member of the Japanese New Leftist group Chūkakuha was arrested and charged as the suspected perpetrator, but was later acquitted, with the ruling becoming final.

Many surviving examples were painted on paper and used in religious fundraising campaigns.

Both temples and shrines (and their deities, [[:en:Deity|kami]] and {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhas|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仏陀|1=Buddha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7055}}) have been objects of faith in Japan, and prior to the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], they were often treated collectively without distinction when not necessary.

{{ill|Miyadaiku|ja|宮大工|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyadaiku|1=miyadaiku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65261787}} (redirect from {{ill|Temple and Shrine Carpenter|ja|寺社大工|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Carpenter|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮大工|1=miyadaiku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65261787}}) Miyadaiku refers to master carpenters specializing in the construction and restoration of temples and shrines. These craftsmen maintain unique traditional techniques, including the use of yari kanna (a spear-plane), which has largely disappeared from general carpentry. Only a handful of specialist companies, such as Takigawa Jisha Architecture in Nara, are entrusted with the restoration of National Treasure and Important Cultural Property buildings.
{{ill|Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records|ja|大乗院寺社雑事記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records|1=Daijōin Jisha Zōjiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432101}} The Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records (だいじょういんじしゃぞうじき) is a diary written over three generations by Jinson, Shōkaku, and Kyōjin, monks who managed Kōfuku-ji’s Daijōin in the Muromachi period. Spanning approximately 190 volumes, the surviving records from 1450 (Hōtoku 2) to 1527 (Daiei 7) are preserved in the National Archives of Japan and designated as Important Cultural Property.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Power|ja|寺社勢力|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Power|qq=}} The Temple and Shrine Power (じしゃせいりょく) refers to the military, administrative, economic, and cultural influence of large temples and shrines in medieval Japan. Alongside the military government and the imperial court, religious institutions played a central role in politics and governance. Due to the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, many major religious sites wielded substantial territorial control and maintained private armies.
{{ill|Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|ja|京都寺社等同時放火事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|1=Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11374949}} The Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident was a series of arson attacks that took place in Kyoto on April 24-25, 1993. A senior member of the Japanese New Leftist group Chūkakuha was arrested and charged as the suspected perpetrator, but was later acquitted, with the ruling becoming final.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala|ja|社寺参詣曼荼羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala|qq=}} A Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala is a religious painting created to depict sacred sites, such as temples and shrines, for the purpose of promoting pilgrimage and religious devotion. Many surviving examples were painted on paper and used in religious fundraising campaigns.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Honjoryō|ja|寺社本所領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Honjoryō|1=jisha-honjo-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458054}} Temple and Shrine Honjoryō refers to the estates belonging to religious institutions in contrast to those controlled by the warrior class. Before the 12th century, there was no clear distinction, but as the Kamakura shogunate strengthened its authority, temple and shrine lands were increasingly categorized separately from warrior-held territories.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Regulations|ja|寺社法度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Regulations|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺院諸法度|1=Jiin shohatto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5361074}} Temple Regulations were a set of legal codes used by the Edo shogunate and local lords to govern religious institutions. These laws often granted temple and shrine lands while imposing various controls, such as restrictions on logging within temple and shrine precincts.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|ja|寺社興行法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|1=Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458055}} The Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws refer to legal measures implemented by secular authorities to restore and regulate religious ceremonies. These laws aimed to revive religious practices that had declined due to various circumstances, particularly from the late Heian period onward.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Law|ja|寺社法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Law|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺院法}} Temple Law, also known as Temple and Shrine Law, refers to the legal framework governing religious institutions in premodern Japan. This framework involved two main types of authority: secular control over religious institutions and self-regulation by the religious communities themselves. == See Also == * [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] * {{ill|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Authorities|ja|寺社勢力|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Authorities|qq=}} * [[:en:Jisha-bugyō|Magistrates for Temples and Shrines]] * [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] * [[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Japan|List of Temples in Japan]] * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Japan|List of Shinto shrines in Japan]]

<!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--> [[Category:Buddhist temples]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[zh:寺社]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11458051}} [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社]][[zh:寺社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社]][[zh:寺社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社]][[zh:寺社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11458051]] Wikidata name: jisha [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Original research|date=January 2011}} '''Temples and Shrines''' (寺社, Jisha) is a collective term in Japan referring to both [[:en:Buddhist temple|temples]] and [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrines]]. It is also called '''Shrines and Temples''' (社寺, Shaji) or '''Shrines and Temple Precincts''' (神社仏閣, Jinja Bukkaku).
The term "Jisha" reflects the idea of Buddhist supremacy over Shinto that prevailed until the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. On the other hand, the term "Shaji" appeared as early as the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], primarily reflecting a preference for shrines and was widely used after the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]].
== Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism ==
Both temples and shrines (and their deities, [[:en:Deity|kami]] and {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhas|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仏陀|1=Buddha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7055}}) have been objects of faith in Japan, and prior to the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], they were often treated collectively without distinction when not necessary.
The history of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] lies in the blending of native [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] beliefs and imported [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] (especially [[:en:Mikkyō|Esoteric Buddhism]]), which gave rise to diverse faiths like [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]]. Theoretical frameworks like the [[:en:Honji suijaku|honji suijaku]] doctrine attempted to unify these practices, with "Gongen" (権現) serving as an ambiguous term representing both. Examples of architectural integration include "Jingūji" (神宮寺, Shrine-Temples).
During the Meiji period's separation policies, syncretized entities were forcibly divided into either shrines or temples, often resulting in the abolition of one side.
However, at the grassroots level, these changes caused minimal shifts in popular consciousness. Local [[:en:Folk religion|folk religions]] and {{ill|festivals|ja|祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/festivals|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祭|1=festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132241}} continued relatively unchanged, preserving traditions beyond the scope of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]].
In modern times, the differences between temples and shrines are seldom emphasized outside specific rituals like funerals. Japanese people are often described as having a "weak sense of faith," with temples and shrines viewed more for their {{ill|worldly benefits|ja|現世利益|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/worldly benefits|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 現世|1=this world|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20183021}} or as [[:en:Tourist attraction|tourist destinations]].
==Articles with this term in their title== [[:en:Jisha-ryō|Temple and Shrine Estates]] Temple and Shrine Estates (じしゃりょう) refer to one of the territorial divisions that existed in Japan in the past. Primarily, these estates were established to support the maintenance and operation of temples and shrines. In ancient times, revenue sources for temples and shrines included self-owned lands (shinden and teraden), grants from the ritsuryō state, and early shōen estates developed through reclamation efforts. Some major temples and shrines held extensive territories that provided significant income.
[[:en:Jisha-bugyō|Temple and Shrine Magistrate]] The Temple and Shrine Magistrate (じしゃぶぎょう) was an official position within the military governments from the Muromachi period through the Edo period, responsible for overseeing religious administration. Originating in the Kamakura shogunate, this office managed temple and shrine estates, buildings, monks, and shrine priests. The Edo shogunate established this position in 1635 (Kan'ei 12), placing it under the direct authority of the shogun and Ranking it highest among the three magistrates. The magistrate also oversaw other cultural and administrative aspects related to religious institutions.
{{ill|Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line|ja|新潟県道590号保田寺社線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line|1=Niigata Prefectural Road Route 590|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16484359}} Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line (にいがたけんどう590ごう やすだじしゃせん) is a general prefectural road in Agano City, Niigata Prefecture. It serves as an access route from National Route 49 to the Niigata Eastern Industrial Complex. - Starting point: Yasuda, Agano City, Niigata Prefecture (intersection with Niigata Prefectural Route 55) - Endpoint: Jisha, Agano City (intersection with National Route 49)
{{ill|Miyadaiku|ja|宮大工|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyadaiku|1=miyadaiku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65261787}} (redirect from {{ill|Temple and Shrine Carpenter|ja|寺社大工|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Carpenter|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮大工|1=miyadaiku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65261787}}) Miyadaiku refers to master carpenters specializing in the construction and restoration of temples and shrines. These craftsmen maintain unique traditional techniques, including the use of yari kanna (a spear-plane), which has largely disappeared from general carpentry. Only a handful of specialist companies, such as Takigawa Jisha Architecture in Nara, are entrusted with the restoration of National Treasure and Important Cultural Property buildings.
{{ill|Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records|ja|大乗院寺社雑事記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records|1=Daijōin Jisha Zōjiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432101}} The Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records (だいじょういんじしゃぞうじき) is a diary written over three generations by Jinson, Shōkaku, and Kyōjin, monks who managed Kōfuku-ji’s Daijōin in the Muromachi period. Spanning approximately 190 volumes, the surviving records from 1450 (Hōtoku 2) to 1527 (Daiei 7) are preserved in the National Archives of Japan and designated as Important Cultural Property.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Power|ja|寺社勢力|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Power|qq=}} The Temple and Shrine Power (じしゃせいりょく) refers to the military, administrative, economic, and cultural influence of large temples and shrines in medieval Japan. Alongside the military government and the imperial court, religious institutions played a central role in politics and governance. Due to the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, many major religious sites wielded substantial territorial control and maintained private armies.
{{ill|Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|ja|京都寺社等同時放火事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|1=Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11374949}} The Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident was a series of arson attacks that took place in Kyoto on April 24-25, 1993. A senior member of the Japanese New Leftist group Chūkakuha was arrested and charged as the suspected perpetrator, but was later acquitted, with the ruling becoming final.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala|ja|社寺参詣曼荼羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala|qq=}} A Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala is a religious painting created to depict sacred sites, such as temples and shrines, for the purpose of promoting pilgrimage and religious devotion. Many surviving examples were painted on paper and used in religious fundraising campaigns.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Honjoryō|ja|寺社本所領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Honjoryō|1=jisha-honjo-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458054}} Temple and Shrine Honjoryō refers to the estates belonging to religious institutions in contrast to those controlled by the warrior class. Before the 12th century, there was no clear distinction, but as the Kamakura shogunate strengthened its authority, temple and shrine lands were increasingly categorized separately from warrior-held territories.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Regulations|ja|寺社法度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Regulations|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺院諸法度|1=Jiin shohatto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5361074}} Temple Regulations were a set of legal codes used by the Edo shogunate and local lords to govern religious institutions. These laws often granted temple and shrine lands while imposing various controls, such as restrictions on logging within temple and shrine precincts.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|ja|寺社興行法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|1=Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458055}} The Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws refer to legal measures implemented by secular authorities to restore and regulate religious ceremonies. These laws aimed to revive religious practices that had declined due to various circumstances, particularly from the late Heian period onward.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Law|ja|寺社法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Law|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺院法}} Temple Law, also known as Temple and Shrine Law, refers to the legal framework governing religious institutions in premodern Japan. This framework involved two main types of authority: secular control over religious institutions and self-regulation by the religious communities themselves. == See Also == * [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] * {{ill|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Authorities|ja|寺社勢力|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Authorities|qq=}} * [[:en:Jisha-bugyō|Magistrates for Temples and Shrines]] * [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] * [[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Japan|List of Temples in Japan]] * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Japan|List of Shinto shrines in Japan]]

These laws aimed to revive religious practices that had declined due to various circumstances, particularly from the late Heian period onward.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Law|ja|寺社法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Law|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺院法}} Temple Law, also known as Temple and Shrine Law, refers to the legal framework governing religious institutions in premodern Japan. This framework involved two main types of authority: secular control over religious institutions and self-regulation by the religious communities themselves. == See Also == * [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] * {{ill|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Authorities|ja|寺社勢力|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Authorities|qq=}} * [[:en:Jisha-bugyō|Magistrates for Temples and Shrines]] * [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] * [[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Japan|List of Temples in Japan]] * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Japan|List of Shinto shrines in Japan]]

<!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples--><!--Shrines and Temples-->

{{wikidata link|Q11458051}} [[Category:Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Temples]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社]][[zh:寺社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社]][[zh:寺社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:寺社]][[zh:寺社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11458051]] Wikidata name: jisha [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Original research|date=January 2011}} '''Temples and Shrines''' (寺社, Jisha) is a collective term in Japan referring to both [[:en:Buddhist temple|temples]] and [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrines]]. It is also called '''Shrines and Temples''' (社寺, Shaji) or '''Shrines and Temple Precincts''' (神社仏閣, Jinja Bukkaku).
The term "Jisha" reflects the idea of Buddhist supremacy over Shinto that prevailed until the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. On the other hand, the term "Shaji" appeared as early as the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], primarily reflecting a preference for shrines and was widely used after the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]].
== Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism ==
Both temples and shrines (and their deities, [[:en:Deity|kami]] and {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhas|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仏陀|1=Buddha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7055}}) have been objects of faith in Japan, and prior to the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], they were often treated collectively without distinction when not necessary.
The history of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] lies in the blending of native [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] beliefs and imported [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] (especially [[:en:Mikkyō|Esoteric Buddhism]]), which gave rise to diverse faiths like [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]]. Theoretical frameworks like the [[:en:Honji suijaku|honji suijaku]] doctrine attempted to unify these practices, with "Gongen" (権現) serving as an ambiguous term representing both. Examples of architectural integration include "Jingūji" (神宮寺, Shrine-Temples).
During the Meiji period's separation policies, syncretized entities were forcibly divided into either shrines or temples, often resulting in the abolition of one side.
However, at the grassroots level, these changes caused minimal shifts in popular consciousness. Local [[:en:Folk religion|folk religions]] and {{ill|festivals|ja|祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/festivals|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祭|1=festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132241}} continued relatively unchanged, preserving traditions beyond the scope of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]].
In modern times, the differences between temples and shrines are seldom emphasized outside specific rituals like funerals. Japanese people are often described as having a "weak sense of faith," with temples and shrines viewed more for their {{ill|worldly benefits|ja|現世利益|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/worldly benefits|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 現世|1=this world|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20183021}} or as [[:en:Tourist attraction|tourist destinations]].
==Articles with this term in their title== [[:en:Jisha-ryō|Temple and Shrine Estates]] Temple and Shrine Estates (じしゃりょう) refer to one of the territorial divisions that existed in Japan in the past. Primarily, these estates were established to support the maintenance and operation of temples and shrines. In ancient times, revenue sources for temples and shrines included self-owned lands (shinden and teraden), grants from the ritsuryō state, and early shōen estates developed through reclamation efforts. Some major temples and shrines held extensive territories that provided significant income.
[[:en:Jisha-bugyō|Temple and Shrine Magistrate]] The Temple and Shrine Magistrate (じしゃぶぎょう) was an official position within the military governments from the Muromachi period through the Edo period, responsible for overseeing religious administration. Originating in the Kamakura shogunate, this office managed temple and shrine estates, buildings, monks, and shrine priests. The Edo shogunate established this position in 1635 (Kan'ei 12), placing it under the direct authority of the shogun and Ranking it highest among the three magistrates. The magistrate also oversaw other cultural and administrative aspects related to religious institutions.
{{ill|Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line|ja|新潟県道590号保田寺社線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line|1=Niigata Prefectural Road Route 590|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16484359}} Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line (にいがたけんどう590ごう やすだじしゃせん) is a general prefectural road in Agano City, Niigata Prefecture. It serves as an access route from National Route 49 to the Niigata Eastern Industrial Complex. - Starting point: Yasuda, Agano City, Niigata Prefecture (intersection with Niigata Prefectural Route 55) - Endpoint: Jisha, Agano City (intersection with National Route 49)
{{ill|Miyadaiku|ja|宮大工|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyadaiku|1=miyadaiku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65261787}} (redirect from {{ill|Temple and Shrine Carpenter|ja|寺社大工|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Carpenter|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮大工|1=miyadaiku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65261787}}) Miyadaiku refers to master carpenters specializing in the construction and restoration of temples and shrines. These craftsmen maintain unique traditional techniques, including the use of yari kanna (a spear-plane), which has largely disappeared from general carpentry. Only a handful of specialist companies, such as Takigawa Jisha Architecture in Nara, are entrusted with the restoration of National Treasure and Important Cultural Property buildings.
{{ill|Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records|ja|大乗院寺社雑事記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records|1=Daijōin Jisha Zōjiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432101}} The Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records (だいじょういんじしゃぞうじき) is a diary written over three generations by Jinson, Shōkaku, and Kyōjin, monks who managed Kōfuku-ji’s Daijōin in the Muromachi period. Spanning approximately 190 volumes, the surviving records from 1450 (Hōtoku 2) to 1527 (Daiei 7) are preserved in the National Archives of Japan and designated as Important Cultural Property.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Power|ja|寺社勢力|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Power|qq=}} The Temple and Shrine Power (じしゃせいりょく) refers to the military, administrative, economic, and cultural influence of large temples and shrines in medieval Japan. Alongside the military government and the imperial court, religious institutions played a central role in politics and governance. Due to the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, many major religious sites wielded substantial territorial control and maintained private armies.
{{ill|Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|ja|京都寺社等同時放火事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|1=Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11374949}} The Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident was a series of arson attacks that took place in Kyoto on April 24-25, 1993. A senior member of the Japanese New Leftist group Chūkakuha was arrested and charged as the suspected perpetrator, but was later acquitted, with the ruling becoming final.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala|ja|社寺参詣曼荼羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala|qq=}} A Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala is a religious painting created to depict sacred sites, such as temples and shrines, for the purpose of promoting pilgrimage and religious devotion. Many surviving examples were painted on paper and used in religious fundraising campaigns.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Honjoryō|ja|寺社本所領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Honjoryō|1=jisha-honjo-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458054}} Temple and Shrine Honjoryō refers to the estates belonging to religious institutions in contrast to those controlled by the warrior class. Before the 12th century, there was no clear distinction, but as the Kamakura shogunate strengthened its authority, temple and shrine lands were increasingly categorized separately from warrior-held territories.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Regulations|ja|寺社法度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Regulations|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺院諸法度|1=Jiin shohatto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5361074}} Temple Regulations were a set of legal codes used by the Edo shogunate and local lords to govern religious institutions. These laws often granted temple and shrine lands while imposing various controls, such as restrictions on logging within temple and shrine precincts.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|ja|寺社興行法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|1=Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458055}} The Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws refer to legal measures implemented by secular authorities to restore and regulate religious ceremonies. These laws aimed to revive religious practices that had declined due to various circumstances, particularly from the late Heian period onward.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Law|ja|寺社法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Law|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺院法}} Temple Law, also known as Temple and Shrine Law, refers to the legal framework governing religious institutions in premodern Japan. This framework involved two main types of authority: secular control over religious institutions and self-regulation by the religious communities themselves. == See Also == * [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] * {{ill|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Authorities|ja|寺社勢力|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Authorities|qq=}} * [[:en:Jisha-bugyō|Magistrates for Temples and Shrines]] * [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] * [[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Japan|List of Temples in Japan]] * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Japan|List of Shinto shrines in Japan]]

==Articles with this term in their title== [[:en:Jisha-ryō|Temple and Shrine Estates]] Temple and Shrine Estates (じしゃりょう) refer to one of the territorial divisions that existed in Japan in the past. [[:en:Jisha-bugyō|Temple and Shrine Magistrate]] The Temple and Shrine Magistrate (じしゃぶぎょう) was an official position within the military governments from the Muromachi period through the Edo period, responsible for overseeing religious administration. Originating in the Kamakura shogunate, this office managed temple and shrine estates, buildings, monks, and shrine priests. The Edo shogunate established this position in 1635 (Kan'ei 12), placing it under the direct authority of the shogun and Ranking it highest among the three magistrates. The magistrate also oversaw other cultural and administrative aspects related to religious institutions.
{{ill|Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line|ja|新潟県道590号保田寺社線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line|1=Niigata Prefectural Road Route 590|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16484359}} Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line (にいがたけんどう590ごう やすだじしゃせん) is a general prefectural road in Agano City, Niigata Prefecture. It serves as an access route from National Route 49 to the Niigata Eastern Industrial Complex. - Starting point: Yasuda, Agano City, Niigata Prefecture (intersection with Niigata Prefectural Route 55) - Endpoint: Jisha, Agano City (intersection with National Route 49)
{{ill|Miyadaiku|ja|宮大工|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyadaiku|1=miyadaiku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65261787}} (redirect from {{ill|Temple and Shrine Carpenter|ja|寺社大工|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Carpenter|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮大工|1=miyadaiku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65261787}}) Miyadaiku refers to master carpenters specializing in the construction and restoration of temples and shrines. Only a handful of specialist companies, such as Takigawa Jisha Architecture in Nara, are entrusted with the restoration of National Treasure and Important Cultural Property buildings.
{{ill|Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records|ja|大乗院寺社雑事記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records|1=Daijōin Jisha Zōjiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432101}} The Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records (だいじょういんじしゃぞうじき) is a diary written over three generations by Jinson, Shōkaku, and Kyōjin, monks who managed Kōfuku-ji’s Daijōin in the Muromachi period. {{ill|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Power|ja|寺社勢力|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Power|qq=}} The Temple and Shrine Power (じしゃせいりょく) refers to the military, administrative, economic, and cultural influence of large temples and shrines in medieval Japan. Due to the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, many major religious sites wielded substantial territorial control and maintained private armies.
{{ill|Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|ja|京都寺社等同時放火事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|1=Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11374949}} The Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident was a series of arson attacks that took place in Kyoto on April 24-25, 1993. {{ill|Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|ja|寺社興行法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|1=Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458055}} The Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws refer to legal measures implemented by secular authorities to restore and regulate religious ceremonies. Examples of architectural integration include "Jingūji" (神宮寺, Shrine-Temples).
During the Meiji period's separation policies, syncretized entities were forcibly divided into either shrines or temples, often resulting in the abolition of one side.
However, at the grassroots level, these changes caused minimal shifts in popular consciousness. Local [[:en:Folk religion|folk religions]] and {{ill|festivals|ja|祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/festivals|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祭|1=festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132241}} continued relatively unchanged, preserving traditions beyond the scope of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]].
In modern times, the differences between temples and shrines are seldom emphasized outside specific rituals like funerals. Japanese people are often described as having a "weak sense of faith," with temples and shrines viewed more for their {{ill|worldly benefits|ja|現世利益|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/worldly benefits|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 現世|1=this world|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20183021}} or as [[:en:Tourist attraction|tourist destinations]].
==Articles with this term in their title== [[:en:Jisha-ryō|Temple and Shrine Estates]] Temple and Shrine Estates (じしゃりょう) refer to one of the territorial divisions that existed in Japan in the past. Primarily, these estates were established to support the maintenance and operation of temples and shrines. In ancient times, revenue sources for temples and shrines included self-owned lands (shinden and teraden), grants from the ritsuryō state, and early shōen estates developed through reclamation efforts. Some major temples and shrines held extensive territories that provided significant income.
[[:en:Jisha-bugyō|Temple and Shrine Magistrate]] The Temple and Shrine Magistrate (じしゃぶぎょう) was an official position within the military governments from the Muromachi period through the Edo period, responsible for overseeing religious administration. Originating in the Kamakura shogunate, this office managed temple and shrine estates, buildings, monks, and shrine priests. The Edo shogunate established this position in 1635 (Kan'ei 12), placing it under the direct authority of the shogun and Ranking it highest among the three magistrates. The magistrate also oversaw other cultural and administrative aspects related to religious institutions.
{{ill|Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line|ja|新潟県道590号保田寺社線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line|1=Niigata Prefectural Road Route 590|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16484359}} Niigata Prefectural Route 590 Yasuda-Jisha Line (にいがたけんどう590ごう やすだじしゃせん) is a general prefectural road in Agano City, Niigata Prefecture. It serves as an access route from National Route 49 to the Niigata Eastern Industrial Complex. - Starting point: Yasuda, Agano City, Niigata Prefecture (intersection with Niigata Prefectural Route 55) - Endpoint: Jisha, Agano City (intersection with National Route 49)
{{ill|Miyadaiku|ja|宮大工|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyadaiku|1=miyadaiku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65261787}} (redirect from {{ill|Temple and Shrine Carpenter|ja|寺社大工|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Carpenter|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮大工|1=miyadaiku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65261787}}) Miyadaiku refers to master carpenters specializing in the construction and restoration of temples and shrines. These craftsmen maintain unique traditional techniques, including the use of yari kanna (a spear-plane), which has largely disappeared from general carpentry. Only a handful of specialist companies, such as Takigawa Jisha Architecture in Nara, are entrusted with the restoration of National Treasure and Important Cultural Property buildings.
{{ill|Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records|ja|大乗院寺社雑事記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records|1=Daijōin Jisha Zōjiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432101}} The Daijōin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records (だいじょういんじしゃぞうじき) is a diary written over three generations by Jinson, Shōkaku, and Kyōjin, monks who managed Kōfuku-ji’s Daijōin in the Muromachi period. Spanning approximately 190 volumes, the surviving records from 1450 (Hōtoku 2) to 1527 (Daiei 7) are preserved in the National Archives of Japan and designated as Important Cultural Property.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Power|ja|寺社勢力|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Power|qq=}} The Temple and Shrine Power (じしゃせいりょく) refers to the military, administrative, economic, and cultural influence of large temples and shrines in medieval Japan. Alongside the military government and the imperial court, religious institutions played a central role in politics and governance. Due to the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, many major religious sites wielded substantial territorial control and maintained private armies.
{{ill|Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|ja|京都寺社等同時放火事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|1=Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11374949}} The Kyoto Simultaneous Temple and Shrine Arson Incident was a series of arson attacks that took place in Kyoto on April 24-25, 1993. A senior member of the Japanese New Leftist group Chūkakuha was arrested and charged as the suspected perpetrator, but was later acquitted, with the ruling becoming final.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala|ja|社寺参詣曼荼羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala|qq=}} A Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage Mandala is a religious painting created to depict sacred sites, such as temples and shrines, for the purpose of promoting pilgrimage and religious devotion. Many surviving examples were painted on paper and used in religious fundraising campaigns.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Honjoryō|ja|寺社本所領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Honjoryō|1=jisha-honjo-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458054}} Temple and Shrine Honjoryō refers to the estates belonging to religious institutions in contrast to those controlled by the warrior class. Before the 12th century, there was no clear distinction, but as the Kamakura shogunate strengthened its authority, temple and shrine lands were increasingly categorized separately from warrior-held territories.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Regulations|ja|寺社法度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Regulations|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺院諸法度|1=Jiin shohatto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5361074}} Temple Regulations were a set of legal codes used by the Edo shogunate and local lords to govern religious institutions. These laws often granted temple and shrine lands while imposing various controls, such as restrictions on logging within temple and shrine precincts.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|ja|寺社興行法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|1=Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458055}} The Temple and Shrine Restoration Laws refer to legal measures implemented by secular authorities to restore and regulate religious ceremonies. These laws aimed to revive religious practices that had declined due to various circumstances, particularly from the late Heian period onward.
{{ill|Temple and Shrine Law|ja|寺社法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Law|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺院法}} Temple Law, also known as Temple and Shrine Law, refers to the legal framework governing religious institutions in premodern Japan. This framework involved two main types of authority: secular control over religious institutions and self-regulation by the religious communities themselves. == See Also == * [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] * {{ill|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Authorities|ja|寺社勢力|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Authorities|qq=}} * [[:en:Jisha-bugyō|Magistrates for Temples and Shrines]] * [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] * [[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Japan|List of Temples in Japan]] * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Japan|List of Shinto shrines in Japan]]
